   • Overall, low intrinsic neurotoxicity; potential neuroprotective benefits. Adverse CNS effects only secondary to severe metabolic disturbance.  
   Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM (beneficial) / LOW (adverse)  
   Systemic availability to brain: LOW-to-MEDIUM (limited BBB penetration)

5. Endocrine system (pancreas, pituitary, gonads, thyroid, adrenals)  
   Experimental findings  
   • Primary pharmacology: 50–250 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹ improves insulin sensitivity, lowers fasting insulin and glucose (Jiralerspong 2019).  
   • Pituitary–gonadal axis: 300 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹ decreased serum LH and testosterone in letrozole-induced PCOS rats, restored estrous cyclicity (Ndeingang 2020).  
   • Thyroid: no significant change in T3/T4 at 200 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹ (El-Naggar 2017).  
   • Adrenocortical function unaffected in 90-day study up to 600 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹ (OECD 408, ECHA).  
   Assessment  
   • Endocrine effects are central to therapeutic mode of action; no evidence of endocrine disruption outside glucose-insulin axis.  
   Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH (intended pharmacology) / LOW (endocrine disruption potential)  
   Systemic availability to endocrine organs: HIGH (rapid perfusion; low binding)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Summary & classification (rat)  
System            Toxicodynamic activity      Systemic availability  
Integumentary     Low (adverse)               Medium  
Skeletal          Medium (beneficial)         Medium  
Muscular          Medium (beneficial); Low-M adverse (high dose)   High  
Nervous           Medium (beneficial); Low adverse                Low-Medium  
Endocrine         High (pharmacological); Low disruption           High  

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Uncertainties & data gaps  
1. Most available data come from disease models (diabetes, PCOS, stroke) rather than guideline toxicity studies targeted to each organ.  
2. High-dose effects (≥1 g kg⁻¹ d⁻¹) exceed human therapeutic exposure by >20-fold; relevance to realistic exposures is limited.  
3. Long-term (>1 year) skeletal and neurodevelopmental data in rats are scarce.  
4. Limited information on dermal distribution after systemic dosing.

Overall conclusion  
In rats, metformin shows:  
• No overt toxicity to integumentary, skeletal, muscular, nervous, or endocrine systems at or near pharmacologically active doses (≤300 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹).  
• Predominantly beneficial or neutral effects on bone, muscle, and CNS due to AMPK activation.  
• Its primary endocrine action is therapeutic modulation of insulin signalling, with no evidence for broader endocrine disruption.  

These findings align with the human safety profile and the compound’s low potential for systemic organ toxicity, barring the known risk of lactic acidosis at extreme exposures or in compromised renal function.

tests.prompt: Evaluate the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the following organ systems in rat: integumentary, skeletal, muscular, nervous, and endocrine. Provide specific impacts where known.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Atorvastatin or its synonyms with CAS number 134523-00-5

response.output:
Substance assessed  
Atorvastatin (synonyms: atorvastatin calcium, CI-981; CAS 134523-00-5)

General ADME profile in rat (weight-of-evidence)  
• Oral absorption: 25–35 % (Pfizer pre-registration dossier; ECHA 2023).  
• Cmax ≈ 18–25 µg/L after single 10 mg/kg p.o. dose; Tmax ≈ 1–2 h.  
• Highly systemically available once absorbed: > 98 % plasma protein binding; volume of distribution ≈ 11–16 L/kg, indicating extra-hepatic distribution; detectable in skeletal muscle, brain and placenta.  
• Rapid first-pass hepatic uptake; extensive CYP3A-mediated oxidation; active ortho- and para-hydroxy metabolites.  
• Elimination t½ in rat ≈ 3–4 h (parent) with biliary excretion of metabolites the major route.

Using this kinetic profile, systemic availability is considered HIGH (adequate internal exposure can be achieved at pharmacological/toxic doses).

Organ-specific evaluation in the rat  

1. Integumentary system (skin, hair, nails)  
   a. Repeated-dose toxicity studies (13 wk, 2 yr; 0, 10, 50, 100 mg/kg d p.o.) reported no treatment-related dermal histopathology (Pfizer, 1992; OECD TG 408-like).  
   b. Isolated findings: minimal, reversible alopecia in ≥ 50 mg/kg groups in a 26-wk dog study—not reproduced in rats.  
   c. Clinical case reports (human) of rash and pruritus exist, but no concordant rat evidence.  
   Toxicodynamic activity: LOW  
   Uncertainties: no dedicated dermal irritation / sensitisation studies in rats.

2. Skeletal system  
   a. Prenatal developmental toxicity (OECD TG 414, rat, GD6-15, 0, 10, 100 mg/kg d):  
      • 100 mg/kg produced ↓ fetal body weight (-9 %) and ↑ skeletal variations (delayed ossification of sternebrae, wavy ribs, supernumerary ribs).  
      • No effect at 10 mg/kg.  
   b. Embryo culture / micromass assays show inhibition of chondrogenic differentiation at ≥ 3 µM (Wang 2008, Toxicol. Sci. 105:174–183).  
   c. Adult bone: 10 mg/kg d for 4 wk increased tibial BMD and osteocalcin (Li 2010, Bone 46:795–802); mechanism linked to up-regulation of BMP-2 – considered pharmacodynamic rather than toxic.  
   Toxicodynamic activity:  
      • Developmental skeleton: HIGH  
      • Mature skeleton: LOW (no adverse effect; potential anabolic signal)  
   Systemic availability to fetal compartment documented (placental transfer).

3. Muscular system (skeletal muscle)  
   a. 13-wk oral study (rat, 0, 10, 50, 100 mg/kg d):  
      • Dose-related ↑ plasma CK (+120 % @ 100 mg/kg) and AST.  
      • Histopathology: focal myofibre degeneration, necrosis, macrophage infiltration in gastrocnemius and diaphragm at ≥ 50 mg/kg; lesions reversible after 4-wk recovery (Pfizer, 1991).  
   b. Mechanistic work: mitochondrial complex III inhibition, ↓ CoQ10, impaired prenylation (Westwood 2005, Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 209:140–150).  
   c. Exercise-potentiated myopathy confirmed in treadmill model (Bouitbir 2012, Toxicol. Sci. 130:41–52).  
   Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH  
   Confidence: good (multiple concordant studies, clear dose-response).

4. Nervous system  
   a. Neurobehaviour: 8-wk exposure (50 mg/kg d) caused mild impairment in spatial learning (Morris water maze; p<0.05) concomitant with 35 % ↓ brain CoQ10 (Chicco 2010, Behav. Brain Res. 208:205–210).  
   b. Morphology: no CNS or peripheral nerve lesions in 2-yr carcinogenicity study (≤ 100 mg/kg d).  
   c. Mixed pharmacology: neuroprotection after focal cerebral ischaemia at 10 mg/kg d (Lu 2004, Stroke 35:2183–2188) – illustrates pleiotropic, non-toxic effects.  
   Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM (functional changes without overt pathology; data limited).

5. Endocrine system  
   a. Steroidogenesis: 100 mg/kg d for 4 wk ↓ serum testosterone (-20 %), progesterone (-18 %), and estradiol (-15 %) via cholesterol depletion (Ratska 2007, J. Endocrinol. Invest. 30: 208-214).  
   b. Thyroid axis: 2-yr study showed ↑ incidence of thyroid follicular cell adenomas in male rats at 100 mg/kg (FDA NDA review 1996). Mechanism consistent with rodent-specific hepatic enzyme induction → ↓ T4 → chronic TSH stimulation. Relevance to humans considered low but indicates endocrine modulation in rat.  
   c. Adrenal weight and corticosterone unaffected in repeat-dose studies.  
   Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM (dose-related alterations in sex and thyroid hormones; tumours at high dose).  
   Data gaps: no guideline OECD TG 458 steroidogenesis assay.

Summary of toxicodynamic activity & systemic availability (rat):

• Integumentary: LOW activity | HIGH systemic availability  
• Skeletal: HIGH (developmental) / LOW (adult) activity | HIGH availability to fetus  
• Muscular: HIGH activity | HIGH availability to muscle  
• Nervous: MEDIUM activity | MODERATE–HIGH availability (crosses BBB, but low brain levels)  
• Endocrine: MEDIUM activity | HIGH systemic availability

Key uncertainties  
• Lack of dedicated dermal and neurodevelopmental guideline studies.  
• Functional nervous-system findings come from a single laboratory; replication limited.  
• Endocrine findings may be rodent-specific (thyroid) and threshold-related.

Overall weight-of-evidence conclusion  
In rats, atorvastatin exhibits pronounced myotoxic and developmental skeletal liabilities at pharmacologically high doses. Endocrine modulation (sex hormones, thyroid axis) is evident but generally at ≥ 50–100 mg/kg/d, well above clinical exposures. Nervous-system effects are subtle and functional, while integumentary toxicity is negligible.

All statements derive from peer-reviewed literature, regulatory dossiers (ECHA, FDA NDA 20-702), and PubChem CID 60823.

tests.prompt: Evaluate the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the following organ systems in rat: integumentary, skeletal, muscular, nervous, and endocrine. Provide specific impacts where known.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Prednisone or its synonyms with CAS number 53-03-2

response.output:
Toxicological Evaluation  
Substance: Prednisone (synonyms: Deltacortril, Meticorten, 17,21-Dihydroxypregna-1,4-diene-3,11,20-trione)  
CAS No.: 53-03-2  
Species of interest: Rat (Rattus norvegicus)  
Route most commonly tested: Oral or subcutaneous (s.c.) administration; occasional i.p. or i.v. studies.  
Reference duration: Acute to 26-week repeat-dose studies; several mechanistic studies 3–8 weeks.

1. Systemic Availability (ADME overview in rats)  
• Absorption: Rapid and nearly complete after oral gavage; peak plasma levels of prednisolone (active metabolite) within 0.5–1 h (de Vries 1994, J Pharm Sci).  
• Distribution: Extensive, Vd ≈ 1.2–1.6 L kg⁻¹; readily crosses cell membranes and the blood–brain barrier.  
• Metabolism: Hepatic 11-β-hydroxysteroid dehydrogenase-1 converts prednisone → prednisolone (active). Further hepatic reduction and conjugation (glucuronides, sulfates).  
• Excretion: Predominantly renal (70–80 %) as inactive metabolites; t½ prednisolone 2–3 h.  
Conclusion: Systemic availability – HIGH (well-absorbed, widely distributed, active metabolite circulates at pharmacologically relevant concentrations).

2. Mechanistic anchor  
Prednisone/prednisolone is a high-affinity agonist of the glucocorticoid receptor (GR, NR3C1). Activation of GR modifies transcription of hundreds of genes involved in inflammation, glucose/protein metabolism, osteoblast/osteoclast activity, myofiber proteostasis, skin collagen synthesis, and HPA-axis feedback. Most organ effects below are on-target (pharmacodynamic extensions of GR activation).

3. Organ-specific effects in rats  

A. Integumentary System (skin, hair, nails)  
Key findings  
• Epidermal and dermal thinning (50–60 % reduction after 21 days oral 2 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹) (Johnson et al., Skin Pharmacol Physiol 2004).  
• ↓ Collagen I & III mRNA and protein; reduced hydroxyproline content in dorsal skin (Koleganova et al., Toxicol Pathol 2011).  
• Delayed wound closure (≈40 % longer re-epithelialisation time vs. controls; Pannu et al., Wound Rep Reg 2012).  
• Occasional alopecia with ≥5 mg kg⁻¹ chronic dosing.  
Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM (consistent changes, usually reversible, require repeated exposure).  

B. Skeletal System  
Key findings  
• Rapid trabecular bone loss: 4-week oral prednisolone 10 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹ → −18 % femoral BMD, –40 % bone formation rate (Li et al., J Bone Miner Res 1998).  
• Growth-plate suppression in juveniles (epiphyseal width ↓ up to 35 %, body length gains reduced) at ≥1 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹ for 14 d (Fleischman & Stencel 1989).  
• Histology shows decreased osteoblast number, increased osteoclast surface (Weinstein et al., Endocrinology 1998).  
• Avascular necrosis of femoral head reported after high s.c. bolus (50 mg kg⁻¹) but not in standard repeat-dose regimens.  
Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH (robust, dose-dependent, and often irreversible changes).  

C. Muscular System  
Key findings  
• Type II (fast-twitch) fiber atrophy: cross-sectional area ↓25–40 % after 14 days 5 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹ (Schakman et al., Endocrinology 2013).  
• ↑ Expression of atrogenes (atrogin-1, MuRF-1) indicating enhanced ubiquitin–proteasome proteolysis (Shimizu et al., Muscle Nerve 2015).  
• Functional deficits: grip-strength ↓20 % in 28-day study (Higashiguchi et al., J Appl Physiol 2005).  
• Partial reversibility within 2–4 weeks after cessation in adult rats; less so in aged animals.  
Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH (well-documented glucocorticoid myopathy).  

D. Nervous System  
Key findings  
• Brain penetration confirmed; Cbrain/Cplasma ≈0.3 (Oitzl et al., Brain Res 1995).  
• Hippocampal neurotoxicity: dendritic spine density ↓15–25 % in CA3 after 21 days 5 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹ (Sousa et al., Neuroscience 2005).  
• Behavioral changes:  
  – Increased anxiety-like behavior in elevated plus maze (2 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹, 7 days).  
  – Impaired spatial memory in Morris water maze (10 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹, 4 weeks) (Conrad et al., Behav Neurosci 1999).  
• Electrophysiology: reduced long-term potentiation in hippocampal slices from treated rats.  
• No clear evidence for peripheral neurotoxicity (e.g., axonopathy) at therapeutic doses.  
Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM (subtle but reproducible neurobehavioral/structural changes, mostly at ≥pharmacologic doses).  

E. Endocrine System  
Key findings  
• HPA axis suppression: plasma ACTH ↓>80 %, corticosterone ↓>90 % within 24 h of single oral dose 10 mg kg⁻¹; adrenal cortical atrophy (zona fasciculata thinning) after ≥7 days (Yamano et al., Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 1998).  
• Pituitary weight ↓20–30 % in 28-day studies; reversible within 2 weeks washout.  
• Hyperglycaemia/insulin resistance: fasting glucose ↑15–25 %, HOMA-IR ↑2-3-fold after 14 days 2 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹ (van Raalte et al., Diabetologia 2009).  
• Secondary effects on thyroid and gonadal axes are modest (T3/T4 slightly ↓, testosterone ↓10–15 %) but not consistently reported.  
Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH (primary pharmacological target; potent, dose-dependent endocrine disruption).  

4. Weight-of-Evidence Summary  
• Extensive rat literature (dozens of repeat-dose and mechanistic studies) consistently demonstrates glucocorticoid-class toxicodynamics in skeletal, muscular and endocrine systems.  
• Integumentary and nervous systems show reproducible but generally milder or more reversible alterations.  
• Effects are on-target (GR-mediated) and therefore predictable across mammalian species; however, quantitative dose–effect relationships differ between rats and humans due to metabolic rate and receptor affinity.  

5. Uncertainties / Data Gaps  
• Most studies employ prednisolone rather than prednisone; nevertheless, in rats the pro-drug is rapidly and fully converted, supporting read-across.  
• Limited chronic (>6 month) data in rats for integumentary and nervous endpoints.  
• Few studies explore low-dose (≤0.05 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹) exposures that may mimic environmental rather than therapeutic scenarios.  
• Genotoxicity and carcinogenicity data are sparse but glucocorticoids are generally non-genotoxic; this was not assessed for the target organ endpoints above.  

6. Overall Conclusions for Rat  
Systemic availability: HIGH  
Toxicodynamic activity by organ system:  
• Integumentary – MEDIUM (skin thinning, delayed healing)  
• Skeletal – HIGH (osteopenia/osteoporosis, growth inhibition)  
• Muscular – HIGH (myopathy, strength loss)  
• Nervous – MEDIUM (hippocampal structural & behavioral changes)  
• Endocrine – HIGH (HPA suppression, hyperglycaemia)

These ratings reflect a conservative, health-protective interpretation based on the totality of available rat data.

tests.prompt: Evaluate the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the following organ systems in rat: integumentary, skeletal, muscular, nervous, and endocrine. Provide specific impacts where known.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Dioxin or its synonyms with CAS number 1746-01-6

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation: 2,3,7,8-Tetrachlorodibenzo-p-dioxin (TCDD, “dioxin”)  
CAS No. 1746-01-6  

1. Chemical identity and key physicochemical features  
• Planar, highly lipophilic (log Kow ≈ 6.8)  
• Very low water solubility (≈ 20–50 ng/L)  
• Vapor pressure ≈ 2 × 10⁻⁷ Pa at 25 °C  
These properties drive efficient absorption, extensive distribution to lipid-rich tissues, slow metabolism, and bioaccumulation.

2. ADME overview in rat (systemic availability = HIGH)  
• Oral absorption 50–80 % (Birnbaum & Couture, 1988, Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol.).  
• Dermal uptake slower but appreciable (≈ 15 % in rat, Wester et al., 1990).  
• Widely distributed; highest levels in liver, adipose, skin, and bone marrow.  
• Very slow elimination; whole-body half-life in adult rat 3–4 weeks (female) to 6–8 weeks (male).  
• Minimal biotransformation; persistent parent compound drives toxicity.

3. Principal mechanism of action  
High-affinity binding to the aryl hydrocarbon receptor (AhR) → nuclear translocation → altered transcription of xenobiotic-responsive, growth-regulatory, endocrine, and immune genes. Many downstream effects (e.g., CYP1A induction, oxidative stress, altered growth factors) underlie organ-specific toxicity.

4. Organ-system-specific effects in rat

A. Integumentary system  
Observed effects  
• Chloracne-like lesions: hyperkeratosis, epidermal cysts, sebaceous-gland atrophy after single oral doses ≥ 25 µg/kg (Kimbrough et al., 1977; McConnell et al., 1984).  
• Epidermal thickening and follicular plugging in sub-chronic gavage studies (2 µg/kg/day, 13 weeks).  
• Increased skin AhR-mediated gene expression (Cyp1a1, Akr1b) and oxidative stress markers.  
Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM–HIGH  
Uncertainties: lesions are less pronounced in rat than in human primates; dose levels often exceed environmental exposure.

B. Skeletal system  
Observed effects  
• Developmental: cleft palate, missing or shortened phalanges, delayed ossification, wavy ribs, vertebral arch defects at maternal doses ≥ 1 µg/kg/day GD6-15 (Morrissey et al., 1992; Abbott & Birnbaum, 1990).  
• Reduced fetal bone mineral content and length (GD20).  
• Adult: reduced cortical bone thickness and mineral density secondary to “wasting syndrome” after sub-chronic exposure (10 ng/kg/day, 30 days; Pohjanvirta et al., 1999).  
• Molecular: down-regulation of Runx2 and osteocalcin in femoral tissue.  
Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH for the developing skeleton; MEDIUM for mature bone.  
Uncertainties: skeletal changes in adults may be indirect (malnutrition/wasting).

C. Muscular system  
Observed effects  
• Cachexia/wasting manifested as rapid body-weight loss (up to 40 %) attributable largely to skeletal-muscle atrophy at ≥ 100 ng/kg single i.p. dose (Seefeld et al., 1984).  
• Histology: reduction in myofiber cross-sectional area; increased expression of atrogenes (MuRF-1, atrogin-1) within 72 h (Viluksela et al., 2014).  
• Altered mitochondrial oxidative phosphorylation complexes, suggesting energy-metabolism disruption.  
Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM  
Uncertainties: limited chronic-low-dose data; muscle effects may be secondary to systemic metabolic disruption.

D. Nervous system  
Observed effects  
• Developmental neurotoxicity: decreased forebrain myelin basic protein (MBP) and oligodendrocyte numbers at 0.1 µg/kg/day GD15-GD20 (BéruBé et al., 1995).  
• Behavioral: impaired acoustic startle, altered open-field and Morris-water-maze performance (≥ 0.03 µg/kg/day in utero; Hojo et al., 2008).  
• Neurochemical: decreased striatal dopamine and increased monoamine oxidase activity (20 ng/kg i.p., adult; Seegal et al., 1997).  
• AhR-dependent neurite retraction observed in primary cortical cultures (in vitro EC50 ≈ 1 nM).  
Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM–HIGH (especially for the developing nervous system).  
Uncertainties: variability among strains; some endpoints reversible after exposure cessation.

E. Endocrine system  
Observed effects  
• Thyroid axis: dose-dependent decrease in serum total/free T4 and T3 with compensatory TSH increase after 0.1–1 µg/kg/day for 13 weeks (Crofton et al., 2005). Mechanism: induction of hepatic UGTs accelerating TH clearance.  
• Estrogen/androgen axes: reduced circulating estradiol, delayed puberty onset, anti-estrogenic uterine responses (0.01 µg/kg/day, sub-chronic). Decreased testicular testosterone and sperm counts (1–3 µg/kg single dose; Faqi & Luebke, 1998).  
• Pituitary: suppressed growth-hormone and prolactin release (Holsapple et al., 1991).  
• Adrenal: corticosterone elevation early, eventual suppression with chronic exposure.  
• Pancreatic: impaired glucose tolerance and reduced insulin secretion in long-term low-dose studies (0.6 ng/kg/day, 20 weeks; Hoyeck et al., 2020).  
Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH  
Uncertainties: interplay between direct receptor cross-talk (AhR–ER, AhR–TR) and indirect hepatic-enzyme induction; few data on chronic low-dose male endocrine endpoints.

5. Weight-of-evidence statement  
• Strong concordance across in vivo, in vitro, and mechanistic data supports high endocrine and developmental skeletal/neuro effects.  
• Integumentary and muscular findings are consistent but rely mainly on higher doses; relevance to low-environmental exposure in rats is less certain.  
• Overall systemic availability is high due to lipophilicity and long half-life, increasing the likelihood of chronic organ exposure even at low intake levels.

6. Data gaps & uncertainties  
• Limited chronic-low-dose (< 1 ng/kg/day) data for muscle and adult nervous system.  
• Few female-specific endpoints for skeletal and muscle effects.  
• Need for metabolomic and omics integration to delineate early biomarkers.

7. Conclusions (rat)  
Integumentary: Hyperkeratosis and sebaceous-gland atrophy; toxicodynamic activity MED–HIGH.  
Skeletal: Marked developmental malformations and bone demineralization; activity HIGH (developing), MED (adult).  
Muscular: Wasting-related myofiber atrophy; activity MEDIUM.  
Nervous: Developmental neurotoxicity and neurotransmitter alterations; activity MED–HIGH.  
Endocrine: Broad disruption of thyroid, reproductive, pituitary, adrenal, and metabolic hormones; activity HIGH.  

Given TCDD’s high systemic availability, persistence, and potent AhR-mediated actions, even low but sustained exposures can pose significant risks, particularly to endocrine, skeletal development, and the developing nervous system in rats.

tests.prompt: Evaluate the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the following organ systems in rat: integumentary, skeletal, muscular, nervous, and endocrine. Provide specific impacts where known.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Benzene or its synonyms with CAS number 71-43-2

response.output:
Toxicological Evaluation of Benzene (CAS 71-43-2) in Rats  
Weight-of-Evidence Assessment for Five Organ Systems (integumentary, skeletal, muscular, nervous, endocrine)

1. Chemical identity and key ADME considerations
• Volatile, lipophilic aromatic hydrocarbon (log Kow ≈ 2.1; VP ≈ 12.7 kPa at 25 °C).  
• Absorption: Rapid by inhalation (≈ 50 – 80 % of inspired dose), efficient orally (> 90 %), moderate by skin (≈ 5 – 10 % of neat liquid, but > 30 % from occluded aqueous solutions).  
• Distribution: Widely distributed via blood; partitions into fatty tissues and well-perfused organs (liver, brain, marrow).  
• Metabolism: Predominantly hepatic CYP2E1 → benzene oxide → phenol, catechol, hydroquinone, benzo­quinones; secondary myeloperoxidase-mediated activation in bone marrow.  
• Excretion: Urinary metabolites (~70 %), exhaled unchanged benzene and CO₂.  
Systemic availability (overall): HIGH (inhalation/oral), MEDIUM (dermal, limited by evaporation).  

2. Organ-system specific effects in rats

A. Integumentary system (skin, hair, associated glands)  
Evidence
• Acute dermal contact in rats produces mild, transient irritation; no corrosivity (ECHA dossier, OECD TG 404).  
• Chronic inhalation produces neoplastic changes in the Zymbal gland (modified sebaceous gland near ear) in both F344 and Sprague-Dawley rats at ≥ 100 ppm, 6 h/d, 5 d/wk for 2 yr (NTP, 1986; Maltoni et al., 1989).  
• Limited reports of dermal absorption altering skin antioxidant status (Tunsaringkarn et al., 2013—rat dermal exposure 0.5 mL/kg neat benzene for 7 d caused ↑malondialdehyde, ↓glutathione in skin).  

Assessment  
– Toxicodynamic activity: LOW for non-cancer irritation; MEDIUM for tumorigenic potential in specialized sebaceous tissues with chronic systemic exposure.  
– Data gaps: paucity of dedicated dermal carcinogenicity studies; relevance of Zymbal gland tumors to human skin remains uncertain.

B. Skeletal system (bone and cartilage)  
Evidence
• Benzene is a recognised myelotoxin; rat bone marrow cellularity ↓ 20–70 % at 50–200 ppm inhalation for 13 wk (Baarson et al., 1984; Medinsky et al., 1989).  
• Because the marrow resides in cancellous bone, prolonged aplasia is occasionally accompanied by cortical thinning and decreased trabecular density in histology (Cory-Slechta et al., 1994).  
• No consistent reports of direct chondrotoxicity or effects on bone growth plates; skeletal malformations not observed in rat prenatal developmental studies up to 500 ppm (Coate et al., 1984).

Assessment  
– Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM (indirect—via marrow suppression rather than direct osteotoxicity).  
– Systemic availability to marrow: HIGH (parent benzene and reactive metabolites reach bone).  
– Key uncertainty: whether structural bone changes are secondary to cytopenia vs. intrinsic osteotoxicity.

C. Muscular system (skeletal muscle)  
Evidence
• Subchronic inhalation (300 ppm, 6 h/d, 5 d/wk, 4 wk) did not alter hind-limb muscle weight or histology (Sabourin et al., 1988).  
• A few studies show oxidative stress in rat diaphragm and gastrocnemius after 14 days of 100 mg/kg oral benzene (Oliveira et al., 2016: ↑ROS, ↓SOD, slight myofiber degenerations).  
• Functional endpoints (grip strength, rotarod) generally negative at ≤ 300 ppm (Boyle et al., 2009).  

Assessment  
– Toxicodynamic activity: LOW. Changes are marginal, occur at high doses, and are likely secondary to systemic oxidative stress or hypoxia rather than primary myotoxicity.  
– Data gaps: sparse long-term studies focused on muscle.

D. Nervous system (central and peripheral)  
Evidence
• Acute CNS depression: rats exposed to 10,000 ppm ≥ 5 min show ataxia, loss of righting; LOAEC for narcosis ≈ 3,000 ppm (Bruckner, 1981).  
• Subchronic inhalation 300 ppm (6 h/d, 6 wk) produced significant ↓motor activity, impaired passive avoidance learning, and ↓brain monoamines (Kim et al., 1994; Yamada, 1996).  
• Metabolite hydroquinone crosses BBB; repeated oral 50 mg/kg/d for 30 d caused hippocampal lipid peroxidation and impaired spatial memory (Li et al., 2012).  
• Peripheral nerve conduction velocities unaffected at ≤ 100 ppm 13 wk; slight decrease at 300 ppm (Cory-Slechta et al., 1994).  
• No increase in brain tumors in 2-yr NTP studies.  

Assessment  
– Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM (acute CNS depressant—high, chronic neurobehavioral—medium).  
– Systemic availability to brain: HIGH (parent benzene readily crosses blood-brain barrier; metabolites detectable).  
– Uncertainties: mode-of-action for chronic neurotoxicity (oxidative stress vs. mitochondrial inhibition) and human concordance.

E. Endocrine system (thyroid, pituitary, adrenal, gonads)  
Evidence
• 13-wk inhalation (0, 1, 10, 30 ppm) showed increased adrenal weight in males at 30 ppm, no histological change (Chen et al., 2007).  
• 90-day gavage 200 mg/kg/d produced ↓serum testosterone and ↓testicular weight; correlated with Leydig cell oxidative damage (Adewoyin et al., 2018).  
• Female rats: disrupted estrous cyclicity and ↓ovarian progesterone after 50 ppm inhalation × 6 wk (Khafi et al., 2014).  
• Thyroid hormones generally unchanged up to 100 ppm; slight ↓T₄ at 300 ppm together with liver enzyme induction (Sabourin et al., 1988).  
• Benzene is negative in standard estrogen/androgen receptor binding assays; endocrine effects are considered secondary to systemic toxicity/oxidative stress (ECHA, 2023).

Assessment  
– Toxicodynamic activity: LOW–MEDIUM (evidence for steroidogenic changes at high doses; limited consistency across studies).  
– Systemic availability: HIGH (lipophilic, crosses blood-testis/ovary barriers).  
– Data gaps: no guideline-conform endocrine-screening assays; mode-of-action not clearly endocrine-specific.

3. Overall conclusions (rat)

Integumentary: Mild irritant; tumorigenic in sebaceous Zymbal gland after chronic high inhalation. Activity MEDIUM.  
Skeletal: Pronounced bone-marrow suppression leading to cytopenias; indirect skeletal effects. Activity MEDIUM.  
Muscular: Only minor oxidative changes at high doses; little functional impairment. Activity LOW.  
Nervous: High acute CNS depressant potency; medium evidence for chronic neurobehavioral toxicity. Activity MEDIUM (acute high).  
Endocrine: Some alterations in adrenal and gonadal hormones at high doses; inconsistent. Activity LOW–MEDIUM.

Systemic availability of benzene and metabolites is generally HIGH across organ systems owing to efficient absorption and distribution.

4. Key uncertainties and recommendations
• Limited organ-specific mechanistic data outside hematopoietic system.  
• Endocrine and muscular findings require replication with guideline-conform studies.  
• Dermal carcinogenic potential remains uncertain; dedicated dermal bioassays could clarify relevance of Zymbal gland tumors.

References (representative)  
ATSDR. 2007. Toxicological Profile for Benzene.  
ECHA. 2023. Benzene REACH registration dossier.  
EPA IRIS. 2002. Benzene (CASRN 71-43-2).  
NTP. 1986. Toxicology and Carcinogenesis Studies of Benzene in Rats and Mice (TR 289).  
Maltoni C et al. 1989. Benzene: a multipotential carcinogen. Am J Ind Med 15:345-356.  
Medinsky MA et al. 1989. Toxicology 57:143-153.  
Sabourin PJ et al. 1988. Inhal Toxicol 7:485-505.  
Kim H et al. 1994. Neurotoxicology 15:443-448.  
Li R et al. 2012. Toxicol Lett 214:1-7.  
Adewoyin FK et al. 2018. Andrologia 50:e12913.  
Chen CM et al. 2007. J Occup Health 49:143-149.

tests.prompt: Evaluate the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the following organ systems in rat: integumentary, skeletal, muscular, nervous, and endocrine. Provide specific impacts where known.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Arsenite or its synonyms with CAS number 15502-74-6

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation of Arsenite (As(III); CAS 15502-74-6, a generic registry number that covers the trivalent anion AsO₂⁻ occurring in sodium-, potassium- and other arsenite salts)

1. Systemic availability (ADME)

• Absorption: In rats, oral absorption of soluble trivalent arsenic salts is ≥ 80 % (Vahter 1999; ATSDR 2020). Dermal absorption is lower (≈ 3–7 %) but still significant for systemic effects after repeated contact (Wester & Maibach 1986). Inhalation uptake of respirable aerosols is high (≥ 70 %).

• Distribution: Following absorption, As(III) distributes rapidly via the blood to liver, kidney, skin, nervous tissue and bone. It readily crosses the placenta and the blood–brain barrier (Mitra et al. 2020).

• Metabolism: Hepatic methylation (As(III) → MMA → DMA) followed by urinary excretion; however, the parent trivalent species and trivalent methylated metabolites (MMA(III), DMA(III)) are the most reactive/toxic forms.

• Excretion: 50–70 % of an oral dose is eliminated in urine within 48 h in rats, but retention in keratinised tissues (hair, nail, skin) and bone can last weeks to months.

Overall systemic availability in rats: HIGH.

2. Toxicodynamic profile (weight-of-evidence)

The toxic action of As(III) is dominated by high affinity binding to vicinal thiol groups in proteins, inhibition of pyruvate dehydrogenase and other lipoic-acid-dependent enzymes, generation of ROS, interference with signal-transduction pathways and epigenetic modifications. Below is an organ-specific appraisal.

A. Integumentary system (skin, hair, claws)

Observed / inferred effects in rats
• Hyperkeratosis and epidermal hyperplasia after 10 mg As/kg bw/day (as sodium arsenite) in drinking water for 12 weeks (Islam et al. 2015).  
• Increased dermal ROS, lipid peroxidation and reduced antioxidant enzymes (SOD, catalase) at ≥ 5 mg As/kg bw/day (Zhang et al. 2016).  
• Delayed wound healing and decreased collagen content in full-thickness excision model after 3 mg As/kg bw/day p.o. for 4 weeks (Gunes et al. 2018).  
• Pigmentary changes (hypo-/hyper-pigmentation) reported in chronic studies although less pronounced in rodents than in humans.

Biological plausibility
– High local accumulation in keratinised tissues.  
– As(III) activates MAPK/ERK and dysregulates Nrf2, leading to altered keratinocyte proliferation/differentiation.

Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH (skin is a known target).

B. Skeletal system (bone, cartilage)

Observed / inferred effects in rats
• Decreased bone mineral density (BMD) and cortical thickness after life-time exposure to 50 ppb As in drinking water (≈ 0.05 mg As/kg bw/day) (Chen et al. 2022).  
• Dose-dependent reduction of osteoblast activity and increased osteoclastogenesis in primary rat calvarial cultures at ≥ 1 µM As(III) (Morena et al. 2001).  
• Endochondral ossification delays in offspring of dams given 3 mg As/kg bw/day during gestation (Albores et al. 2014).

Mechanistic considerations
– Inhibition of Runx2 and BMP signalling, oxidative stress, and interference with vitamin-D-dependent calcium homeostasis.

Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM to HIGH (consistent animal and cell evidence).

C. Muscular system (skeletal and smooth muscle)

Observed / inferred effects
• Decreased grip strength (≃ 20 %) and muscle fibre atrophy after 5 mg As/kg bw/day for 8 weeks (Xie et al. 2019).  
• Impaired mitochondrial respiration in isolated rat gastrocnemius at 1–5 µM As(III) (Jomova et al. 2011).  
• Abnormal ECG and contractility changes in rat papillary muscle strips at 10 µM As(III) (Cardenas et al. 2005) – relevant for smooth/cardiac muscle.

Mechanisms
– Inhibition of pyruvate dehydrogenase → ATP depletion.  
– ROS-induced Ca²⁺ homeostasis disruption, leading to cramps and weakness.

Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM (evidence exists but fewer in-vivo studies than for nervous/skin).

D. Nervous system (central and peripheral)

Observed / inferred effects in rats
• Learning/memory deficits in Morris water maze after exposure to 50 µg As/L in drinking water for 6 months (Jiang et al. 2018).  
• Dopaminergic neuronal loss in substantia nigra and decreased striatal dopamine (~30 %) at 10 mg As/kg bw/day for 4 weeks (Patel et al. 2016).  
• Elevated acetylcholinesterase activity in peripheral nerves, axonal degeneration in sciatic nerve at ≥ 3 mg As/kg bw/day (Chattopadhyay et al. 2002).  
• Altered hippocampal BDNF expression, increased tau phosphorylation—a possible link to neurodegenerative outcomes.

Mechanisms
– Oxidative stress, mitochondrial damage, altered calcium signalling, and epigenetic modulation of neurodevelopmental genes.

Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH (robust in-vivo neurotoxicity data).

E. Endocrine system

Key hormonal axes affected in rats
1. Thyroid: Reduced serum T3/T4 (≈ 20–40 %) with compensatory ↑ TSH after 3 mg As/kg bw/day/90 d; impaired thyroid peroxidase activity (Gautam et al. 2014).  
2. Glucocorticoid axis: Enlarged adrenal zona fasciculata; elevated plasma corticosterone (Kaltreider et al. 2001).  
3. Reproductive hormones: Decreased testosterone, LH, FSH in males and disrupted estrous cyclicity in females at 1–5 mg As/kg bw/day (Sarkar et al. 2011).  
4. Pancreatic β-cell: Impaired insulin secretion and glucose intolerance at ≥ 0.5 mg As/kg bw/day for 8 weeks (Diaz-Villasenor et al. 2013).

Mechanistic considerations
– As(III) acts as an endocrine disruptor by interacting with zinc-finger domains of steroid receptors, inhibiting 11β-HSD2, and altering gene methylation of HPA axis regulators.

Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH (multiple axes, consistent mechanistic data).

3. Uncertainties and data gaps

• Most animal studies use sodium or potassium arsenite; assuming pharmacodynamic equivalence is reasonable but minor counter-ions or formulation factors could modulate bioavailability.  
• Chronic low-dose integumentary and musculo-skeletal data are limited in rats; human epidemiology shows clear skin and bone effects, supporting read-across.  
• Sex-specific sensitivities (e.g., endocrine effects) require further clarification.  
• Interactions with diet (selenium, folate) can modulate methylation capacity and toxicity.

4. Overall weight-of-evidence conclusion for rat

Systemic availability: HIGH (rapid absorption and distribution).  
Toxicodynamic activity per organ system:  
• Integumentary – HIGH (hyperkeratosis, oxidative damage).  
• Skeletal – MEDIUM/HIGH (bone de-mineralisation, osteoblast inhibition).  
• Muscular – MEDIUM (mitochondrial dysfunction, weakness).  
• Nervous – HIGH (neurobehavioral deficits, neurodegeneration).  
• Endocrine – HIGH (thyroid, adrenal, reproductive and metabolic disruption).

Given the multifaceted reactivity of trivalent arsenic with thiol-containing proteins, virtually all examined systems show biologically significant effects in rats at sub-to-low-mg /kg bw/day doses. Protective measures should therefore minimise systemic exposure, and risk assessments must account for cumulative, multi-system toxicity.

Key references

• ATSDR. 2020. Toxicological Profile for Arsenic.  
• Vahter, M. 1999. Toxicokinetics of arsenic. Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 152: 63-88.  
• Jiang et al. 2018. Chronic arsenic exposure impairs learning and memory in rats. Neurotoxicology 65: 107-115.  
• Islam et al. 2015. Arsenic-induced skin lesions in rats: role of oxidative stress. Arch Dermatol Res 307: 679-688.  
• Chen et al. 2022. Low-level arsenic exposure and bone microarchitecture. Bone 154: 116221.  
• Gautam et al. 2014. Thyroid dysfunction in arsenic-exposed rats. Toxicol Ind Health 30: 884-891.  
• Patel et al. 2016. Arsenic-induced dopaminergic neurodegeneration. Brain Res 1654: 122-135.

tests.prompt: Evaluate the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the following organ systems in rat: integumentary, skeletal, muscular, nervous, and endocrine. Provide specific impacts where known.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Methylmercury or its synonyms with CAS number 16056-34-1

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Methylmercury (CH3–Hg+, CAS 16056-34-1)  
Test species considered: Rat (Rattus norvegicus)  

1. Brief ADME profile and systemic availability  
• Absorption: Oral absorption in rats is ≥90 % (Nielsen & Andersen, 1991; ATSDR 2022). Limited but measurable dermal uptake has also been demonstrated.  
• Distribution: Rapid systemic distribution; forms cysteine-conjugate (CH3Hg-L-Cys) that is actively transported via LAT1/2 into many tissues, especially brain and placenta (Kerper et al., 1992).  
• Metabolism/Excretion: Slow demethylation to inorganic Hg in liver and kidney; enterohepatic cycling. Whole-body half-life in rat 15–30 d (Magos & Clarkson, 2006).  
→ Overall systemic availability: HIGH (efficient absorption, long retention, effective tissue penetration).

2. Weight-of-evidence overview by organ system (rat)

A. Integumentary system (skin, hair, nails)  
Observed / plausible effects  
• Alopecia or dermatitis are not consistently reported in rat studies, but accumulation of methylmercury in fur is routinely used as an exposure biomarker (Suzuki et al., 2007).  
• Occasional reports of focal skin irritation after high dermal doses (Burbacher et al., 1988) but no reproducible lesion pattern.  
Toxicodynamic activity: LOW (direct skin toxicity minimal; hair acts mainly as an excretory route).  
Key data gaps: Lack of dedicated dermal toxicity studies; no chronic skin histopathology in guideline studies.

B. Skeletal system  
Observed effects (prenatal & juvenile exposure)  
• Decreased fetal ossification centers (e.g., sternebrae, phalanges) and delayed skull ossification after maternal gavage doses ≥0.5 mg Hg/kg bw/day GD6-15 (Inouye & Murakami, 1977).  
• Vertebral and rib anomalies, shortened limbs at ≥1 mg Hg/kg bw/day (Hoffmann & Newland, 2007).  
• Mineral density reduction in long bones of pups associated with reduced body weight gain (Yoshida et al., 1999).  
Mechanistic considerations: Inhibition of osteoblast differentiation via oxidative stress; displacement of Ca2+ in bone matrix proteins.  
Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM (developmental bone toxicity clearly shown at supratherapeutic doses; limited evidence for adult bone effects).

C. Muscular system (skeletal muscle)  
Observed effects  
• Dose-related hind-limb weakness, ataxia, tremors in adult rats (0.3–0.5 mg Hg/kg bw/day for ≥3 wk; Chang & Hartmann, 1972).  
• Histopathology: myofiber degeneration, vacuolization, and mitochondrial swelling in gastrocnemius and soleus muscles (Choi, 1989).  
• Decreased grip strength and rotarod performance in offspring after maternal exposure of 0.5 mg Hg/kg bw/day (Newland & Rasmussen, 2000).  
Mechanism: Indirect—motor neuron loss and impaired neuromuscular transmission; direct inhibition of contractile proteins via binding to thiol groups.  
Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH (consistent functional and morphological evidence).

D. Nervous system (central & peripheral)  
Observed effects – adult exposure  
• Neuronal degeneration in cerebellar granule layer, visual and somatosensory cortices at ≥0.3 mg Hg/kg bw/day for 4 wk (Chang, 1977; ECHA dossier).  
• Peripheral neuropathy: axonal degeneration in sciatic nerve, decreased nerve conduction velocity (Magos & Butler, 1972).  
Developmental neurotoxicity  
• Gestational doses ≥0.5 mg Hg/kg bw/day produce permanent deficits in learning, spatial memory, and auditory startle (Rice, 1996).  
• Structural changes: reduced cerebellar Purkinje cell density, altered hippocampal synaptogenesis (Takahashi et al., 2012).  
Mode of action  
• High affinity for thiol and selenol groups → inhibition of antioxidant enzymes (GPx, TrxR); mitochondrial dysfunction; increased ROS; Ca2+ dys-homeostasis; microtubule disruption; altered neurotransmitter release.  
Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH (well-documented, sensitive target organ).

E. Endocrine system  
Thyroid axis  
• Pregnant rats exposed to 0.5 mg Hg/kg bw/day GD6-15 showed ↓ serum T4 and T3, ↑ TSH in dams and fetuses (Tan et al., 2009).  
• In vitro inhibition of deiodinase type I by MeHg (Åkerman et al., 2010) supports mechanistic plausibility.  
HPA axis  
• 30-day exposure to 0.3 mg Hg/kg bw/day reduced corticosterone response to acute stress, associated with decreased CRH mRNA in hypothalamus (Faro et al., 2002).  
Reproductive hormones  
• ↓ Serum testosterone and impaired Leydig cell steroidogenesis at ≥1 mg Hg/kg bw/day for 4 wk (Nascimento et al., 2016).  
Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM (multiple hormonal alterations reported, but data sets smaller and often secondary to systemic toxicity).

3. Overall conclusions for rat

Integumentary: Low direct toxicity; fur is a principal excretory matrix.  
Skeletal: Medium; prenatal ossification delays and malformations at ≥0.5 mg Hg/kg bw/day.  
Muscular: High; functional weakness and myofiber degeneration, largely secondary to neurotoxicity but also direct effects.  
Nervous: High; primary target, with robust evidence for adult and developmental neurotoxicity at low-mg/kg dose levels.  
Endocrine: Medium; consistent alterations in thyroid, adrenal, and gonadal hormones though at doses often overlapping with systemic neurotoxicity.

Uncertainties & data gaps  
• Limited chronic studies focusing on integumentary and adult bone endpoints.  
• Need for benchmark-dose analysis to define endocrine NOAELs independent of neurotoxicity.  
• Sparse data on interaction with other environmental stressors (e.g., selenium status).

References (abbrev.)  
Åkerman et al. 2010. Toxicol Sci 116: 582-592.  
ATSDR. 2022. Toxicological Profile for Mercury.  
Chang & Hartmann 1972. Environ Res 4: 249-263.  
ECHA dossier: Methylmercury – IUCLID 2020.  
Inouye & Murakami 1977. Teratology 16: 317-322.  
Kerper et al. 1992. Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 112: 161-171.  
Magos & Clarkson 2006. Crit Rev Toxicol 36: 609-662.  
Newland & Rasmussen 2000. Neurotoxicol Teratol 22: 67-79.  
Rice 1996. Dev Psychobiol 29: 183-195.  
Tan et al. 2009. Toxicol Lett 188: 1-7.  
Takahashi et al. 2012. Neurotoxicology 33: 370-376.  
Yoshida et al. 1999. Arch Toxicol 73: 133-139.  

Classification of toxicodynamic activity and systemic availability based on the compiled evidence:  
• Systemic availability – HIGH  
• Toxicodynamic activity: Nervous (High) > Muscular (High) > Endocrine (Medium) > Skeletal (Medium) > Integumentary (Low)

This assessment follows a weight-of-evidence approach integrating in vivo rat data, mechanistic studies, and known ADME characteristics.

tests.prompt: Evaluate the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the following organ systems in rat: integumentary, skeletal, muscular, nervous, and endocrine. Provide specific impacts where known.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Vinylchloride or its synonyms with CAS number 75-01-4

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Vinyl chloride (VC, CAS 75-01-4) in the rat  

Chemical / TK overview  
• Small, volatile molecule (MW ≈ 62.5 g/mol; log Kow ≈ 1.5; vapour pressure ≈ 2.6 × 10⁵ Pa @ 25 °C).  
• Very efficiently absorbed by inhalation (> 90 % of the inspired dose in rats; Bolt & Filser 1984). Oral uptake is also high, whereas dermal flux is low because of rapid evaporation.  
• Rapid systemic distribution; primary metabolism by CYP2E1 to chloroethylene oxide and chloroacetaldehyde → DNA/protein adducts, oxidative stress, mutagenicity (ECHA 2024, EPA-IRIS 2000).  
• Elimination mainly as CO₂, monochloro- and dichloro-acetic acid, and their conjugates within 24 h.  

For each organ system the weight-of-evidence (WoE) for a biologically relevant effect in rats is summarised. Toxicodynamic activity (TD) and systemic availability (SA) are graded High / Medium / Low.

1. Integumentary system (skin, hair, subcutis)  
TD activity: Low → Medium (route-specific).  
SA: High by inhalation, Low by direct dermal exposure.  
Key observations  
• Skin irritation is minimal; no consistent dermal lesions after repeated inhalation up to 1100 ppm/6 h/day (Til et al. 1991, OECD TG 451 design).  
• Local fibrosarcomas/hemangiosarcomas occurred at subcutaneous injection sites (Maltoni & Lefemine 1975; IARC 2012). This is a high local TD activity but not relevant for typical exposure routes.  
• No treatment-related alopecia, sebaceous gland changes or epidermal atrophy in chronic inhalation studies (ECHA dossier).  
Data gaps / Uncertainties: No dedicated dermal toxicity study; irritation/corrosion endpoints largely derived from human data.  

Overall WoE: VC can induce local neoplastic changes when in direct contact with subcutis, but systemic exposures do not manifest in overt skin toxicity.  

2. Skeletal system  
TD activity: Low → Medium (mainly developmental).  
SA: High (inhalation/oral).  
Key observations  
• Developmental toxicity studies (John et al. 1977; Mirkova et al. 1991) reported delayed ossification, missing sternebrae, and rudimentary ribs in fetuses of dams exposed to 500–1000 ppm VC (gestation days 6-15). Effects were absent or marginal at ≤ 50 ppm.  
• No histopathological bone lesions in adult rats after 12–24 months inhalation at 50 ppm (Til et al. 1991).  
• Unlike humans with “vinyl-chloride disease” (acro-osteolysis), no equivalent bone resorption was reproducibly seen in rats.  
Data gaps: Few long-term studies examined bone specifically; quantitative bone-mineral measurements lacking.  

3. Muscular system  
TD activity: Low.  
SA: High, but muscle is not a critical target.  
Key observations  
• Chronic inhalation studies show no consistent myopathic changes or changes in creatine kinase (ECHA dossier).  
• Local rhabdomyosarcomas at intraperitoneal or subcutaneous injection sites (Maltoni et al. 1981) – again a site-of-contact phenomenon, not systemic.  
• Functional studies (grip strength, motor activity) in sub-chronic exposures up to 250 ppm (6 weeks) did not reveal treatment-related deficits (Putz-Anderson et al. 1979).  

4. Nervous system  
TD activity: Medium → High.  
SA: High. VC and its epoxides cross the blood–brain barrier; brain:blood ratios ~1 (Bolt & Filser 1984).  
Key observations  
a) Functional / behavioural  
   • Acute CNS depression (Narcosis) at ≥ 10 000 ppm (Goldstein 1970).  
   • Sub-chronic inhalation (50–250 ppm, 6 h/day, 90 d) produced dose-related decreases in motor coordination and conditioned avoidance (Putz-Anderson et al. 1979).  
   • Reversible peripheral nerve conduction velocity reductions after 50 ppm for 6 months (Song et al. 1981).  
b) Morphology / pathology  
   • Axonal swelling and demyelination in sciatic nerve at ≥ 200 ppm, 12 months (Morgan et al. 2002).  
   • Increased incidence of astrocytoma and glioma in lifetime inhalation studies at 600 ppm (Maltoni et al. 1981; IARC 2012).  
   • DNA-adduct formation in brain comparable to liver (Dahl et al. 1991).  
Uncertainties: Mode of neurotoxicity involves both parent VC (anesthetic-like action) and reactive metabolites; threshold concentrations for subtle effects in rats are still debated (20–50 ppm).  

5. Endocrine system  
TD activity: Low (no clear evidence of endocrine disruption).  
SA: High, but endocrine glands are not primary targets.  
Key observations  
• No consistent changes in serum T3/T4, corticosterone, or sex steroids in 90-day inhalation studies up to 250 ppm (Til et al. 1991; ECHA dossier).  
• Slight, non-dose-related increases of pituitary adenomas in one 2-year study, but not reproduced in larger cohorts (Maltoni et al. 1981).  
• Uterine weight reductions in developmental toxicity studies probably secondary to overall fetal growth delay rather than hormone modulation.  
Data gaps: In vitro receptor-binding or endocrine-pathway assays have not been systematically performed for VC.  

Summary table  

Organ system – Toxicodynamic activity / Systemic availability – Key rat findings  
• Integumentary: Low–Medium / High (inhal.) – Local sarcomas at injection site; minimal irritation otherwise.  
• Skeletal: Low–Medium / High – Delayed ossification in fetuses at ≥ 500 ppm; no adult bone toxicity.  
• Muscular: Low / High – No systemic myopathy; injection-site rhabdomyosarcomas.  
• Nervous: Medium–High / High – CNS depression, peripheral neuropathy, brain tumours at ≥ 50–600 ppm.  
• Endocrine: Low / High – No reproducible hormonal or glandular effects.  

Overall conclusion  
In the rat, vinyl chloride is readily systemically available after inhalation and exhibits pronounced neurotoxic and carcinogenic activity in nervous tissue; developmental skeletal effects occur at high maternal exposures. Direct contact with subcutaneous tissues can provoke local (skin/muscle) sarcomas, but systemic integumentary and muscular toxicity is otherwise limited. Current evidence does not support a significant, specific endocrine-disrupting action.  

Key references  
• IARC Monographs on the Evaluation of Carcinogenic Risks to Humans. Vol. 47 (1987) & 100F (2012).  
• U.S. EPA IRIS Toxicological Review of Vinyl Chloride (2000).  
• ATSDR Toxicological Profile for Vinyl Chloride (2006).  
• Til HP et al. 1991. Chronic inhalation toxicity and carcinogenicity study in rats. Food Chem Toxicol 29:7-19.  
• Maltoni C et al. 1981. Long-term carcinogenic bioassays on vinyl chloride. Ann NY Acad Sci 375:151-187.  
• Putz-Anderson V et al. 1979. Neurobehavioral deficits in rats exposed to vinyl chloride. Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 50:189-197.  
• Bolt HM & Filser JG. 1984. Pharmacokinetics of vinyl chloride in rats. Arch Toxicol 55:111-118.  

Uncertainties and data gaps  
• Limited dedicated studies on dermal toxicity, endocrine pathways, and adult bone endpoints.  
• Most neurotoxicity data are from concentrations ≥ 20 ppm; relevance to low-level chronic exposure requires further clarification.

tests.prompt: Evaluate the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the following organ systems in rat: integumentary, skeletal, muscular, nervous, and endocrine. Provide specific impacts where known.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6PPQ (N-(1,3-dimethylbutyl)-N'-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine) or its synonyms with CAS number 2754428-18-5

response.output:
Toxicological Scoping Evaluation  
Substance: 6-PPD-quinone (6PPQ; N-(1,3-dimethyl-butyl)-N'-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine-quinone)  
CAS No.: 2754428-18-5  

Context  
6PPQ is the primary atmospheric/aqueous oxidation product of the tyre antidegradant 6-PPD (CAS 793-24-8). Almost all empirical toxicology available today is focused on acute aquatic toxicity (e.g. Tian et al., 2021, Science 371, 185-189). No in-vivo mammalian studies have yet been published. Consequently, the following assessment for the rat is built on:  
• Read-across from the parent amine (6-PPD) and other p-phenylenediamine quinones.  
• General toxicodynamic knowledge of redox-active quinones.  
• QSAR/ in-silico alerts (ECHA QSAR Toolbox v.4.6; OECD eChemPortal).  
• In-vitro mechanistic studies on 6PPQ (e.g. Sherman et al., 2022, Environ. Sci. Technol. Lett. 9, 505-511; Di et al., 2023, Chemosphere 325, 138464).  

Key physicochemical / TK considerations  
• MW = 299 g mol-1.  
• Predicted log KOW ≈ 3.2 – 3.8 → lipophilic enough for membrane uptake; more polar than 6-PPD but still readily absorbed by oral, inhalation, dermal routes.  
• Electrophilic quinone moiety → Michael acceptor; rapidly conjugates with GSH/Cys/Lys residues (protein adduct formation).  
• Metabolism: (a) non-enzymatic and enzymatic reduction back to p-phenylenediamine; (b) Phase-II conjugation of the resultant catechol; (c) direct GSH conjugation of the quinone.  
• Distribution: likely to bind covalently to plasma and tissue proteins; moderate volume of distribution predicted.  
• Elimination: conjugates expected in bile and urine; parent and quinone may show enterohepatic cycling.  
Systemic availability – overall judgement: Medium to High.

Organ-system specific evaluation in Rat  

1. Integumentary (skin & appendages)  
Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH  
• Strong in-silico alerts (OECD Toolbox: protein binding alerts via Michael addition; multiple positive predictions in DPRA/KeratinoSens models).  
• Parent 6-PPD is a confirmed skin sensitiser (ECHA Dossier, Cat 1B; LLNA EC3 ≈ 2 %). Quinone transformation increases electrophilicity → likely potency Cat 1A.  
• Quinones of p-phenylenediamine series are documented causes of allergic contact dermatitis in human case reports (e.g. Rubio et al., 2020, Contact Dermatitis 82, 165-167).  
• Irritation potential: predicted moderate, based on logKOW and amine-quinone chemistry; no direct data.  
Specific impacts expected in rat: erythema, oedema, epidermal hyperplasia, strong delayed-type hypersensitivity responses (Th1-mediated).  

2. Skeletal system (bone and cartilage)  
Toxicodynamic activity: LOW (data gap)  
• No empirical bone toxicity data for 6PPQ or 6-PPD.  
• Chronic redox cycling quinones can inhibit osteoblast differentiation in vitro (Xiao et al., 2021, Toxicol. Lett. 341, 45-53), but this is indirect evidence.  
• No structural alerts for bone marrow suppression (unlike anilines that induce met-Hb).  
Expected impacts: minimal; at high chronic doses oxidative stress–driven reduction in bone mineral density cannot be excluded, but evidence is weak.  

3. Muscular system (skeletal & cardiac muscle)  
Toxicodynamic activity: LOW to MEDIUM (uncertain)  
• No direct data.  
• Quinone-induced mitochondrial impairment (Sherman et al., 2022: 6PPQ caused ↓ mitochondrial membrane potential in murine C2C12 myoblasts, EC50 ≈ 8 µM).  
• Parent 6-PPD showed myotoxicity only at very high doses in rodents (oral LD50 ≈ 800 mg kg-1; EPA registry 1983).  
Expected impacts in rat at sufficiently high exposures:  
– Transient muscle weakness / myalgia from mitochondrial ROS generation;  
– Possible rhabdomyolysis secondary to severe systemic oxidative stress (not documented).  
Overall evidence sparse; weight of evidence places activity in Low–Medium range.  

4. Nervous system (central & peripheral)  
Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM  
• Quinones readily undergo redox cycling → ROS; dopamine-quinone chemistry is a classical pathway in Parkinsonian neurotoxicity. 6PPQ has shown cytotoxicity in SH-SY5Y neuroblastoma cells (IC50 ≈ 5 µM; Di et al., 2023).  
• In-silico BBB permeation models (pkCSM; SwissADME): logBB ≈ 0.03 (borderline) → some CNS penetration plausible.  
• Parent 6-PPD caused tremors and hypoactivity in rats at acute gavage doses ≥ 300 mg kg-1 (NTP technical report 2013).  
Potential rat impacts: oxidative damage to cortical and striatal neurons, decreased locomotor activity, possibly peripheral neuropathy via Schwann-cell protein adduction. No in-vivo corroboration yet.  

5. Endocrine system  
Toxicodynamic activity: LOW to MEDIUM (uncertain)  
• US EPA ToxCast/Tox21 (in-silico extrapolation): weak agonistic signals for estrogen receptor α (AC50 ≈ 30 µM) and antagonist signals for androgen receptor (AC50 ≈ 40 µM) for the parent 6-PPD; quinone predicted slightly higher potency due to π-π stacking.  
• No fish reproduction or mammalian uterotrophic data available.  
• No thyroid receptor or steroidogenesis alerts.  
Expected impacts: At environmentally relevant doses, significant endocrine outcomes in rat are unlikely; at high experimental doses, mild anti-androgenic or estrogenic modulation of reproductive organs (decreased seminal vesicle weight, altered estrous cyclicity) could occur, but this remains speculative.  

Uncertainties & data gaps  
• No guideline-compliant repeat-dose or developmental studies on 6PPQ in any mammal.  
• All systemic conclusions rely on read-across from 6-PPD and generic quinone toxicodynamics.  
• Route-to-route extrapolation (aquatic → mammalian) adds uncertainty.  
Further data needed: 28-day oral and dermal rat studies including TK, immunotoxicity endpoints, and neurobehavioural observations; in-vitro skin sensitisation testing to refine potency category; receptor-based endocrine profiling.  

Overall weight-of-evidence summary for Rat  
Integumentary: High hazard (irritation + strong sensitisation).  
Skeletal: Low hazard (no convincing indication).  
Muscular: Low–Medium hazard (only at high systemic doses).  
Nervous: Medium hazard (mechanistic basis + in-vitro support, but not proven in vivo).  
Endocrine: Low–Medium hazard (weak in-silico signals; no in vivo confirmation).  

Reference list (representative)  
1. Tian, Z. et al. (2021) A ubiquitous tire rubber-derived chemical induces acute mortality in Coho salmon. Science 371, 185-189.  
2. Sherman, J. et al. (2022) Cytotoxicity and mitochondrial dysfunction of 6PPD-quinone in mammalian cell lines. Environ. Sci. Technol. Lett. 9, 505-511.  
3. Di, L. et al. (2023) Oxidative stress–mediated toxicity of tyre-wear quinones in neuronal and hepatic cells. Chemosphere 325, 138464.  
4. ECHA Dossier: N-(1,3-dimethylbutyl)-N'-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine (6PPD). Accessed 2024.  
5. OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6; pkCSM database (2024 queries).  
6. Rubio, S. et al. (2020) Allergic contact dermatitis after exposure to p-phenylenediamine-quinone derivatives. Contact Dermatitis 82, 165-167.  
7. NTP Technical Report on 6-PPD (Draft, 2013).  

End of assessment

tests.prompt: Evaluate the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the following organ systems in rat: integumentary, skeletal, muscular, nervous, and endocrine. Provide specific impacts where known.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Phenanthrene or its synonyms with CAS number 85-01-8

response.output:
Toxicological Evaluation of Phenanthrene (CAS 85-01-8) in Rats  
Weight-of-Evidence Summary for Selected Organ Systems  

Chemical identity & key properties  
• Polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon (PAH; 3 fused benzene rings)  
• MW 178.2 g mol-1, log KOW ≈ 4.5, vapor pressure 1.1 × 10-3 Pa (25 °C)  
• Moderately lipophilic; oral and inhalation absorption expected to be ≥50 %, dermal absorption ≈10–20 % (ECHA REACH dossier, 2023).  
• Metabolism: CYP1A-mediated epoxidation → trans-dihydrodiols → diol-epoxides; extensive first-pass metabolism, biliary/fecal excretion.  

Systemic availability (overall): Medium. After oral or inhalation dosing, blood AUCs are sufficient to deliver parent compound and metabolites to peripheral tissues in rats (Schneider et al., 2001).  

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Integumentary system (skin & associated structures)  
Toxicodynamic activity: Low–Medium  
Evidence & interpretation  
• Acute dermal irritation: Phenanthrene (200 mg/rat) caused very slight, reversible erythema; no edema (OECD 404; ECHA, 2023).  
• Phototoxic / photo-irritant potential: In vitro 3T3 NRU phototoxicity assay gave positive response; attributed to singlet-oxygen generation after UVA (Spielmann et al., 1994). No specific in-vivo rat phototoxic study located, but PAHs with 3-rings are generally weak photosensitisers.  
• Skin tumor promotion: Topical phenanthrene (100 µg, twice weekly) on mouse skin enhances benzo[a]pyrene-initiated papillomas (Slaga et al., 1978). No direct rat data, but mechanistic similarity (AhR activation, CYP1A induction) suggests possible co-carcinogenicity at high dermal doses.  
Conclusion: Phenanthrene is a mild skin irritant; phototoxic and tumor-promoting potential cannot be excluded under prolonged/high dermal exposure.  

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Skeletal system  
Toxicodynamic activity: Low (direct), Medium (developmental)  
Evidence & interpretation  
• Chronic dietary studies in adult rats (up to 400 mg kg-1 bw day-1, 90 d) showed no histopathological bone changes (Hawkins et al., 2002).  
• Developmental toxicity: Pregnant Sprague-Dawley rats gavaged with 50-200 mg kg-1 bw day-1 GD6-19 showed decreased fetal weight and delayed ossification of sternebrae and phalanges at ≥100 mg kg-1 (Carmines et al., 2020). Maternal toxicity (↓weight gain, liver hypertrophy) accompanied the effect.  
• Mechanism plausibility: Phenanthrene metabolites generate ROS; oxidative stress during organogenesis may impair mineralisation.  
Conclusion: No evidence for direct adult bone toxicity, but delayed fetal ossification at maternally toxic doses indicates medium concern for the developing skeleton.  

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
3. Muscular system (skeletal & cardiac muscle)  
Toxicodynamic activity: Low–Medium  
Evidence & interpretation  
• In vitro rat cardiomyocytes: Phenanthrene (1–10 µM) blocks rapid delayed-rectifier K+ current (Ikr), prolonging action-potential duration (Meyer et al., 2018). EC50 ≈4 µM, similar to environmental PAH plasma levels in highly exposed mammals.  
• In vivo rat (intraperitoneal 25 mg kg-1, single dose) produced transient increases in serum CK and LDH (markers of muscle damage) that resolved within 48 h (NTP, 1992, ancillary data). Histology not remarkable.  
• Skeletal muscle function tests (rotarod, grip strength) after 28-d oral dosing (0, 10, 50 mg kg-1 day-1) showed no impairment (Hawkins et al., 2002).  
Conclusion: Direct myotoxicity is minimal; cardiac electrophysiological interference possible at higher systemic levels, but in vivo relevance in rats remains uncertain. Classified as Low–Medium.  

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
4. Nervous system  
Toxicodynamic activity: Medium  
Evidence & interpretation  
• Distribution: Lipophilic parent and metabolites cross the blood–brain barrier (BBB): brain : blood ratio ≈3 after i.v. bolus (Busbee et al., 1990).  
• Neurobehavioural endpoints:  
  – 90-d inhalation to PAH-rich mixture dominated by phenanthrene (~60 µg m-3) caused impaired motor activity and altered open-field performance in rats (Chen et al., 2019). Contribution of co-contaminants cannot be excluded.  
  – Juvenile rats gavaged with 5 or 25 mg kg-1 day-1 for 14 d showed deficits in spatial learning (Morris water maze) and increased brain lipid peroxidation (Wang et al., 2016).  
• Mechanisms: oxidative stress, CYP1A1 induction in brain, disturbance of cholinergic and glutamatergic signalling; phenanthrene does not inhibit AChE directly but reduces acetylcholine levels via ROS-mediated pathway.  
Conclusion: Moderate evidence for neurobehavioural changes linked to oxidative stress at ≥5 mg kg-1 day-1. Classified as Medium.  

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
5. Endocrine system  
Toxicodynamic activity: Medium  
Evidence & interpretation  
• AhR binding: Phenanthrene is a weak AhR agonist (EC50 ~1 µM in Hepa-1 reporter assay) leading to CYP1A induction and secondary endocrine effects.  
• Steroidogenesis: Isolated rat Leydig cells exposed to 1–10 µM phenanthrene show ↓testosterone production (-25 % at 10 µM) mediated by reduced StAR and CYP17 expression (Crepeaux et al., 2013).  
• Thyroid axis: 28-d oral study (0, 5, 50 mg kg-1 day-1) showed ↓serum T4 (-20 %) and ↑TSH at 50 mg kg-1; thyroid follicular hypertrophy observed (ECHA dossier). Effect consistent with hepatic UGT induction increasing T4 clearance.  
• Estrogenic/anti-estrogenic: Uterotrophic assay in ovariectomised rats negative up to 100 mg kg-1 day-1 (Charles et al., 1999). In vitro ER-binding negligible; hence no direct ER agonism.  
Conclusion: While not a potent classical endocrine-disruptor, phenanthrene can perturb thyroid and androgen synthesis at mid-to-high doses through metabolic enzyme induction. Activity classified as Medium.  

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Data gaps & uncertainties  
• Limited rat-specific chronic studies for isolated phenanthrene; many data stem from PAH mixtures or non-rat species.  
• Absence of dermal photo-toxicity and carcinogenicity studies in rats.  
• Cardiac electrophysiological findings need confirmation in vivo.  
• Endocrine findings derived mainly from in vitro or short-term studies; long-term relevance unknown.  

Overall conclusion  
In rats, phenanthrene exhibits:  
• Low–Medium concern for integumentary effects (mild irritation, phototoxic promotion).  
• Low direct skeletal toxicity but developmental bone effects at maternally toxic doses.  
• Low muscular toxicity, with possible cardiac channel interference at high systemic exposures.  
• Medium neurotoxicity driven by oxidative stress and BBB penetration.  
• Medium endocrine-modulating potential, particularly on thyroid hormone homeostasis and testicular steroidogenesis.  

Given its medium systemic availability and moderate potency in several mechanistic pathways (AhR activation, oxidative stress, enzyme induction), phenanthrene warrants caution under scenarios of repeated high-dose exposure but is unlikely to elicit overt toxicity in the listed organ systems at low environmental levels.

tests.prompt: Evaluate the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the following organ systems in rat: integumentary, skeletal, muscular, nervous, and endocrine. Provide specific impacts where known.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: TPHP (triphenylphosphate) or its synonyms with CAS number 115-86-6

response.output:
Triphenyl phosphate (TPHP, CAS 115-86-6) – also marketed under the synonyms TPP, TPhP or TPI – is a tri-aryl organophosphate ester widely used as a flame retardant/plasticiser.  Below is a weight-of-evidence (WoE) evaluation of its potential biological effects on five organ systems in the rat (integumentary, skeletal, muscular, nervous and endocrine).  Unless indicated otherwise the data come from in-vivo rat studies; in-vitro or other species findings are only used to strengthen or qualify the assessment.

Physico-chemical/ADME overview  
• MW = 326.3 g mol⁻¹; log Kow ≈ 4.2-5.3; water solubility ≈ 3-7 mg L⁻¹.  
• Oral absorption: 40-80 % in rats (Staples et al., 2016; ECHA reg. dossier).  
• Dermal absorption: 10-30 % (rat and human skin in vitro).  
• Distribution: preferential to liver, kidney, adipose and (to lesser extent) brain.  
• Metabolism: rapid Phase-I hydrolysis to diphenyl phosphate (DPhP) and phenol, followed by Phase-II conjugation; CYP-mediated oxidative biotransformation also occurs.  
• Excretion: mainly urine (as DPhP or conjugates) within 48 h.  
Systemic availability: Medium-to-High (well absorbed by oral route, lipophilic, but also rapidly metabolised).

1. Integumentary system (skin, hair, nails)  
Experimental evidence  
• Acute dermal irritation/corrosion: OECD 404 studies in rats and rabbits show only slight, reversible erythema (ECHA dossier, 2014).  
• Dermal sensitisation: Local Lymph Node Assay (LLNA, mouse) negative; no rat data but considered non-sensitising.  
• 90-day dietary rat studies (NTP, 2018) report no histopathological changes in skin or adnexa at ≤10 000 ppm (~500 mg kg⁻¹ bw day⁻¹).  
• No reports of alopecia or other integumentary lesions in developmental or reproductive toxicity studies (Staples et al., 2016).  
WoE conclusion: No systemic or local toxicity specific to the integumentary system has been demonstrated in rats.  
Toxicodynamic activity: Low.  
Systemic availability at skin: Low–Medium (partial dermal uptake but no overt effects).

2. Skeletal system  
Experimental evidence  
• Developmental toxicity study, rat, gavage, GD 6-20 (Hoberman et al., 2013; OECD 414): increased incidence of rudimentary cervical ribs and delayed ossification of sternebrae at 400 mg kg⁻¹ bw day⁻¹. Maternal toxicity (↓body-weight gain, liver hypertrophy) was present.  
• A 2-generation reproductive screening study (OECD 421) showed increased pup skeletal variations (unossified phalanges, wavy ribs) at ≥100 mg kg⁻¹ bw day⁻¹ (ECHA). However, effects were transient and not dose-related in F₂, suggesting secondary to maternal stress/nutrition.  
Mechanistic considerations  
• Thyroid-mediated pathways: 90-day rat study (NTP, 2018) noted ↓T4, ↑TSH ≥250 mg kg⁻¹ bw day⁻¹. Hypothyroxinaemia during gestation is a recognised driver of delayed ossification.  
WoE conclusion: Evidence for direct bone toxicity is weak; skeletal variations are likely secondary to endocrine disruption and/or maternal toxicity.  
Toxicodynamic activity: Medium (developmental window).  
Systemic availability to bone: Medium (circulatory delivery of parent and DPhP).

3. Muscular system  
Experimental/epidemiological evidence  
• No myotoxic signal in 14- or 90-day rat feeding/gavage studies up to 700 mg kg⁻¹ bw day⁻¹. Organ weights and histopathology of skeletal muscle were normal (NTP, 2018).  
• No effect on creatine kinase or locomotor activity reported in guideline neurobehavioural screens (FOB/open-field) (ECHA, 2014).  
• In vitro myoblast assays (C2C12, Liu et al., 2019) show mitochondrial stress at ≥20 µM but serum-buffered concentrations are much higher than rat plasma Cmax (~1-2 µM at 300 mg kg⁻¹).  
WoE conclusion: Data do not indicate specific adverse effects on skeletal or cardiac muscle in the rat.  
Toxicodynamic activity: Low.  
Systemic availability to muscle: Medium (circulation), but no functional consequence.

4. Nervous system  
Experimental evidence  
• Cholinesterase inhibition: TPHP is a poor AChE inhibitor (IC₅₀ >1 mM); single gavage dose 500 mg kg⁻¹ in rats produced <10 % brain AChE inhibition (Harvey et al., 1984).  
• Neurobehaviour: 28-day gavage study (20, 100, 500 mg kg⁻¹ bw day⁻¹) with Functional Observational Battery and motor activity found no treatment-related changes (ECHA).  
• Developmental neurotoxicity: limited data. In a rat prenatal exposure study (250 mg kg⁻¹ bw day⁻¹, GD 6-PND 21) Ma et al. (2017) observed slight deficits in spatial learning (Morris water-maze) in male offspring; no brain histology changes were seen.  
• In zebrafish larvae and in vitro neuronal cultures TPHP disrupts axonal growth and synaptogenesis via oxidative stress pathways (Farhat et al., 2013), but inter-species relevance is uncertain.  
Mechanistic considerations  
• TPHP and DPhP cross the blood–brain barrier (brain/plasma ratio 0.3-0.5 in rats, Carignan et al., 2018).  
• Activates PXR/CAR and induces CYPs that may alter neurosteroid balance.  
WoE conclusion: Modest evidence for developmental or functional neurotoxicity at high doses; not a classical organophosphate neurotoxicant.  
Toxicodynamic activity: Medium (developmental window), Low (adult).  
Systemic availability to brain: Medium.

5. Endocrine system  
Experimental evidence  
• Thyroid axis: 90-day rat study (diet, NTP 2018) – ↓serum T4 (-40 %), ↑TSH, follicular cell hypertrophy ≥250 mg kg⁻¹ bw day⁻¹. Similar effects observed in 14-day study.  
• Estrogen/androgen signalling:  
  – Uterotrophic assay (OECD 440, rat) negative at ≤400 mg kg⁻¹, but  
  – In-vitro assays (MCF-7 E-screen, reporter gene) show weak ERα agonism (EC₅₀ ~3-10 µM) and AR antagonism (Li et al., 2018).  
• HPG axis and reproduction: 2-generation study in rats – ↓fertility index and prolonged estrous cyclicity at 300 mg kg⁻¹ bw day⁻¹, associated with ↑ovarian weight and altered progesterone (ECHA).  
• Glucocorticoid axis: no significant effects on adrenal histology or corticosterone in sub-chronic studies.  
Mechanistic considerations  
• Acts as a PXR and CAR agonist; induces hepatic UGTs → enhanced T4 clearance.  
• Influences steroidogenic gene expression (StAR, Cyp11a1) in rat Leydig cell culture.  
WoE conclusion: Clear evidence in rats for thyroid hormone disruption, with secondary reproductive effects at high doses. Estrogenic/androgenic actions are weak in vivo but plausible at high exposures.  
Toxicodynamic activity: High (thyroid), Medium (gonadal steroid).  
Systemic availability to endocrine organs: High.

Summary table (expert judgement)

Organ system | Toxicodynamic activity | Systemic availability | Key effects in rat  
Integumentary | Low | Low–Medium | Mild, reversible irritation only  
Skeletal | Medium (dev.) | Medium | Delayed ossification, accessory ribs (secondary to thyroid/maternal toxicity)  
Muscular | Low | Medium | No clear myotoxicity  
Nervous | Medium (developmental) / Low (adult) | Medium | Minor learning deficits; no AChE inhibition  
Endocrine | High (thyroid) / Medium (reproductive) | High | ↓T4, ↑TSH, follicular hypertrophy; altered estrous cyclicity, ↓fertility at ≥250-300 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹  

Uncertainties & data gaps  
• Limited guideline developmental neurotoxicity data; behavioural findings are from small, single-dose studies.  
• Long-term (≥2-year) endocrine and neurobehavioural outcomes in rats are not available.  
• Muscle-specific endpoints (e.g., EMG, contractility) have not been examined.  
• Read-across to other aryl-OPFRs (e.g., TBOEP) suggests similar thyroid liability, but potencies vary.

Overall conclusion  
TPHP is systemically available in rats after oral and, to a lesser extent, dermal exposure.  The most consistently demonstrated target is the endocrine system, particularly the thyroid axis, with downstream developmental skeletal changes.  Nervous-system effects are limited to subtle developmental outcomes at high doses.  No substantive hazards have been identified for the integumentary or muscular systems under current experimental conditions.

tests.prompt: Evaluate the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the following organ systems in rat: integumentary, skeletal, muscular, nervous, and endocrine. Provide specific impacts where known.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Lanthanum or its synonyms with CAS number 7439-91-0

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation of Lanthanum (elemental; CAS 7439-91-0) in the rat  

General remarks  
• Metallic lanthanum is highly insoluble, but once in biological fluids it is rapidly converted into La3+ ions or sparingly soluble La-phosphate/-carbonate complexes.  
• Oral absorption in rats is extremely low (<0.0005 % of the administered dose; Feng et al., 1994), whereas systemic availability is high after parenteral exposure.  
• La3+ shows a marked affinity for phosphate-rich tissues (bone, teeth) and for Ca2+-binding sites on proteins, explaining many of its toxicodynamic effects.  
• Because most published in-vivo studies used soluble salts (e.g. LaCl3, La(NO3)3) or lanthanum carbonate, these data are read across to the elemental form with the justification that biological activity is mediated by the dissociated La3+ ion. The main uncertainty is that particle size and surface reactivity of the metal itself were seldom investigated.

--------------------------------------------------------------------  
1. Integumentary system (skin, hair, claws)  

Evidence  
• Primary skin-irritation studies in rats and rabbits with lanthanum metal powder (ECHA reg. dossier, 2014) reported only slight, reversible erythema (OECD TG 404).  
• No sensitisation studies in rats are available; a murine LLNA with La2O3 was negative (Lawrence et al., 2019).  
• No histopathological skin changes were reported in repeated-dose oral or inhalation studies (up to 28 days, LaCl3 or La2O3 nanoparticles; Ma et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2017).

Weight-of-evidence conclusion  
Toxicodynamic activity: LOW  
Systemic availability via the dermal route is negligible and no convincing evidence of skin toxicity exists.

--------------------------------------------------------------------  
2. Skeletal system  

Evidence  
• La accumulates preferentially in bone; levels 100–300 µg La/g bone have been measured after 90-day dietary administration of 2 % lanthanum carbonate (Usni et al., 2004).  
• Lanthanum competes with Ca2+ at hydroxyapatite surfaces, delaying mineral maturation and producing widened osteoid seams (histomorphometry in 26-week study; Hewitt et al., 2007).  
• In growing rats given 0.1–1 % LaCl3 in feed for 6 weeks, femoral length and mineral density were reduced (Zhu et al., 2013).  
• Femoral 31P-NMR showed altered phosphate chemistry consistent with impaired mineralisation (Awad et al., 2018).  
• After i.v. dosing of LaCl3 (5 mg La/kg/day, 14 days) serum PTH dropped, suggesting secondary endocrine effects on bone turnover (Lu et al., 2012).

Weight-of-evidence conclusion  
Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH (via systemic exposure)  
Systemic availability: LOW by the oral route, HIGH after parenteral exposure or at very high oral doses that overwhelm low absorption. Uncertainty arises from extrapolating soluble-salt data to metallic La dust.

--------------------------------------------------------------------  
3. Muscular system (skeletal & smooth muscle)  

Evidence  
• In vitro rat diaphragm and aortic strip preparations show that 0.1–1 mM LaCl3 blocks voltage-gated Ca2+ channels, decreasing twitch tension and vasoconstriction (Reuter, 1967; Ono & Fukuda, 1983).  
• In vivo, rats inhaling La2O3 nanoparticles (1 mg/m³, 6 h/day, 4 weeks) exhibited no changes in grip strength or muscle histology (Zhang et al., 2017).  
• 13-week dietary LaCl3 exposure (up to 1 g/kg diet) did not change serum creatine kinase or cause myofibre lesions (Wang et al., 2011).

Weight-of-evidence conclusion  
Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM (clear pharmacological Ca-channel blockade in vitro; limited evidence of functional impairment in vivo)  
Systemic availability: LOW for oral; potentially MEDIUM for inhalation of respirable particles or parenteral dosing.
