• Rats given aerosolised or oral prednisone show reduced airway inflammation in asthma models (beneficial).  
Adverse findings  
• 90-day oral 10 mg kg-1 day-1: ↑ incidence of bacterial bronchopneumonia (secondary to immunosuppression) (ECHA, 2023).  
• No direct pulmonary parenchymal toxicity or fibrosis reported in healthy rats up to 15 mg kg-1 day-1.

Predicted effects  
• Risk of opportunistic respiratory infections, impaired wound repair of airway epithelium.

Classification: Toxicodynamic activity LOW–MEDIUM (adverse effects largely secondary to immunosuppression, not direct pulmonotoxicity).

4. Digestive (gastro-hepatic) system  
Experimental evidence  
• 4-week gavage, 5 & 15 mg kg-1 day-1: gastric mucosal erosions/ulcers in 30 % of high-dose animals; ↑ gastric acid output (Suzuki et al., 1985).  
• 13-week study: hepatomegaly (+20 %), ↑ liver glycogen vacuolation, mild ↑ ALT/AST (≤2×) (ECHA). Reversible after 4-week recovery.  
• Prednisolone (read-across) produced fatty liver change and cholestasis at 25 mg kg-1 day-1, 8 weeks (Yamamoto et al., 1990).

Mechanism  
• GC-induced gluconeogenesis, glycogen storage; suppression of prostaglandin synthesis → decreased gastric mucus.

Classification: Toxicodynamic activity MEDIUM (clear gastric and metabolic liver effects at pharmacological doses).

5. Urinary (renal) system  
Experimental evidence  
• 90-day study, 0, 2, 5, 15 mg kg-1 day-1: kidney weight ↑ 10–15 % (adaptive hypertrophy); mild cortical tubular vacuolation at 15 mg kg-1 day-1.  
• Electrolyte changes: ↑ urinary K⁺, ↑ Ca²⁺ excretion; occasional crystalluria (ECHA; Frazier et al., 1983).  
• Functional parameters (BUN, creatinine clearance) within normal range except transient polyuria/polydipsia.

Predicted effects  
• Chronic high-dose exposure may promote nephrolithiasis (hypercalciuria) and exacerbate hypertension-related renal vascular changes.

Classification: Toxicodynamic activity LOW–MEDIUM (mainly functional/electrolyte perturbations, structural damage minimal at studied doses).

Uncertainties & data gaps  
• Most rat studies are ≤13 weeks; limited chronic (>6 month) data for cardiovascular and renal fibrosis progression.  
• Few dedicated inhalation or dermal studies; however, systemic availability expected to drive effects irrespective of route.  
• Limited sex-specific analysis; females may differ in GC metabolism.

Overall hazard profile in rat  
Prednisone exhibits high systemic availability and exerts pronounced immunosuppressive/lympholytic effects, accompanied by dose-dependent cardiovascular, gastric, hepatic and renal alterations. Direct respiratory toxicity is low, but secondary infection risk exists. The weight-of-evidence indicates:

High concern: Lymphatic/immune; Cardiovascular at ≥5 mg kg-1 day-1  
Medium concern: Digestive; Respiratory (secondary); Urinary  
Low concern: Respiratory (direct tissue toxicity)

References (key)  
1. ECHA dossier: Prednisone (CAS 53-03-2). Registration file 2023.  
2. Marty, J.P. et al. (1972) Biopharmaceutics of prednisone in rat. J. Pharm. Sci.  
3. Tobert, J.A. & Newman, C. (1984) Chronic steroid treatment and blood pressure in rats. J. Cardiovasc. Pharmacol.  
4. Kato, T. et al. (1998) Immunotoxicity of prednisone in rats. Toxicology.  
5. Suzuki, K. et al. (1985) Gastric lesions induced by glucocorticoids in rats. Dig. Dis. Sci.  
6. Frazier, K. et al. (1983) Renal effects of high-dose corticosteroids in laboratory animals. Toxicol. Pathol.  
7. IARC Monographs Vol. 100A (2012) – glucocorticoids (background mechanistic info).  

Assessment conducted according to OECD & EFSA weight-of-evidence principles; uncertainties clearly acknowledged.

tests.prompt: Assess the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the cardiovascular, lymphatic, respiratory, digestive, and urinary systems in rat. Describe any known or predicted impacts.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Dioxin or its synonyms with CAS number 1746-01-6

response.output:
Toxicological Assessment  
Substance: 2,3,7,8-Tetrachlorodibenzo-p-dioxin (TCDD, “dioxin”)  
CAS RN: 1746-01-6  

Overall toxicological profile  
• Extremely potent agonist of the aryl hydrocarbon receptor (AhR); downstream transcriptional alterations drive most systemic effects.  
• Highly lipophilic (log Kow ≈ 6.6), essentially insoluble in water, very slowly metabolised; elimination half-life in adult rat ≥10 days (♀) to several weeks (♂).  
• Systemic availability: HIGH (readily absorbed orally and by inhalation; bioaccumulates in adipose tissue).  
• Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH (strong AhR binding; ED50 for acute wasting in rat ≈1 µg kg⁻¹).  

The table below summarises the weight-of-evidence for the requested organ systems in rats.

1. Cardiovascular system  
Experimental data  
• Prenatal exposure (gavage, 0.64–1.6 µg kg⁻¹ d 15 of gestation) produced fetal heart enlargement, septal defects and altered coronary vascularisation (Thackaberry et al., 2005; Iida et al., 2003).  
• Adult rats (i.p., 10 µg kg⁻¹) showed bradycardia, hypotension and reduced baroreflex sensitivity within 24 h, correlated with decreased α-adrenergic tone (Kopf & Walker, 2009).  
• Chronic low-dose exposure (100 ng kg⁻¹ day⁻¹, 13 weeks) caused left-ventricular concentric hypertrophy and elevated BNP mRNA (Sibilia et al., 2019).  
Mode of action  
AhR-mediated transcription (e.g., up-regulation of CYP1A1/1B1, oxidative stress, altered VEGF signalling) interferes with cardiomyocyte differentiation and vascular tone.  
Classification: Impact PROBABLE/HIGH, especially during development; lower but measurable functional effects in adults.

2. Lymphatic / immune system  
Experimental data  
• Single oral dose 1 µg kg⁻¹ caused rapid thymic atrophy (≥40 % weight loss within 4 days) and decreased CD4⁺/CD8⁺ thymocytes (Vos & Beems, 1971; Funatake et al., 2005).  
• Spleen: diminished germinal-centre formation; suppressed IgM and IgG antibody production after SRBC challenge at ≥0.1 µg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ (Holsapple et al., 1986).  
• Decreased NK-cell activity and delayed-type hypersensitivity responses at 0.01-1 µg kg⁻¹ (Moos et al., 2011).  
Mode of action  
AhR-induced disruption of thymocyte maturation, altered cytokine balance (↑IL-10, ↓IL-2, IFN-γ).  
Classification: Impact HIGH (well-documented immunotoxicant; robust dose-response).

3. Respiratory system  
Experimental data  
• In utero exposure (0.064–1 µg kg⁻¹, GD15) delayed saccular-to-alveolar transition in pups; reduced surfactant protein-B mRNA and altered Clara-cell secretory protein (Johansson et al., 1997).  
• Adult rats showed Clara-cell hypertrophy, bronchial epithelial hyperplasia and CYP1A1 induction in lung after 10 µg kg⁻¹ i.p. (Barouki et al., 2007).  
• Increased susceptibility to bacterial pneumonia (Listeria, Klebsiella) linked to impaired alveolar macrophage function at ≥0.1 µg kg⁻¹ (Teske et al., 2007).  
Mode of action  
AhR-driven dysregulation of epithelial differentiation and immune defence; oxidative stress.  
Classification: Impact MEDIUM to HIGH, stronger during development or infection challenge.

4. Digestive system (focus on liver & GI tract)  
Experimental data  
• Hepatotoxicity is the hallmark: single oral dose 10 µg kg⁻¹ causes hepatomegaly (↑liver weight 50–100 %) within 7 days, periportal vacuolar degeneration, bile-duct proliferation, elevated ALT/AST (Kelling et al., 1987; NTP, 2006).  
• Induction of CYP1A2, CYP1A1, UGT1A enzymes at ≥0.05 µg kg⁻¹ (ELADI data).  
• Chronic feeding (1 ng kg⁻¹ day⁻¹, 2 years) promoted cholangiocarcinoma and hepatocellular adenomas (Kociba et al., 1978).  
• Small intestine: villous blunting and reduced nutrient uptake at ≥1 µg kg⁻¹ (Yoshizawa et al., 2010) – less studied than liver.  
Mode of action  
AhR activation → dysregulated lipid homeostasis, oxidative stress, altered bile-acid synthesis.  
Classification: Impact HIGH (liver), MEDIUM (intestinal).

5. Urinary system (kidney)  
Experimental data  
• Limited primary nephrotoxicity. High single doses (≥100 µg kg⁻¹) produced secondary renal tubular degeneration and proteinuria, attributed to systemic cachexia (Viluksela & Pohjanvirta, 1999).  
• Sub-chronic exposure (100 ng kg⁻¹ day⁻¹, 13 weeks) modestly increased kidney weight (+15 %) with minimal histopathology (Sibilia et al., 2019).  
• In utero exposure associated with reduced nephron number (-10 %) and mild glomerulosclerosis in aged offspring (Nishizawa et al., 2005).  
Mode of action  
Indirect—hemodynamic changes, oxidative stress. No clear AhR-specific kidney pathway identified.  
Classification: Impact LOW to MEDIUM; mainly secondary/ developmental.

Weight-of-evidence and uncertainties  
• Cardiovascular, immune and hepatic effects are consistently reproduced across rat strains and laboratories; data quality high.  
• Respiratory developmental data are moderate; adult pulmonary effects less studied.  
• Kidney data are sparse; most changes likely secondary (cachexia, altered perfusion).  
• Most studies use single bolus or short-term exposure; environmentally relevant nanogram-per-kg, chronic inhalation data in rats are limited.  
• Species extrapolation: Rat appears slightly less sensitive than guinea-pig but more sensitive than mouse for many endpoints; human relevance considered high for liver and immune effects by WHO and US-EPA risk assessments.

Conclusion  
TCDD exhibits high systemic availability and high toxicodynamic activity in rats.  
• Cardiovascular: high developmental cardiotoxicity; functional impacts in adults at ≥10 µg kg⁻¹.  
• Lymphatic/immune: pronounced immunosuppression and thymic atrophy at low doses (LOAEL ≈0.1 µg kg⁻¹).  
• Respiratory: impaired lung development, epithelial changes, increased infection risk (medium–high).  
• Digestive: severe hepatotoxicity, enzyme induction and tumor promotion (high); moderate intestinal effects.  
• Urinary: comparatively low direct toxicity; mild secondary or developmental alterations.

Given the strength of the AhR mechanism, these findings are biologically plausible and supported by a substantial body of in vivo data, although some organ-specific gaps (adult lung, kidney) remain.

tests.prompt: Assess the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the cardiovascular, lymphatic, respiratory, digestive, and urinary systems in rat. Describe any known or predicted impacts.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Benzene or its synonyms with CAS number 71-43-2

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Benzene (CAS 71-43-2) in the rat  
(Weight-of-evidence integration of in vivo, in vitro and in silico information)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Basic physicochemical / TK profile relevant for systemic availability
• Volatile, MW = 78.11 g mol-1; log Kow ≈ 2.1; VP ≈ 12.7 kPa (25 °C).  
• ADME (rats):  
  – Absorption: Rapid by inhalation (> 50–60 % retained in lungs at 100–300 ppm), complete after gavage.  
  – Distribution: Quickly reaches richly perfused organs (liver, kidney, spleen, bone-marrow, heart, lung).  
  – Metabolism: Predominantly CYP2E1 → benzene-oxide → phenol, catechol, hydroquinone, 1,4-benzoquinone; metabolites undergo conjugation (S-, GSH-, sulfate).  
  – Excretion: Mostly via urine as phenolic conjugates (≈ 70 %), minor pulmonary exhalation of parent benzene.  
• Result: Systemic availability – HIGH for inhalation and oral exposure.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Mechanistic anchor
Reactive metabolites generate oxidative stress, covalent binding, topoisomerase II inhibition, chromosomal breakage and altered cytokine signalling. These molecular initiating events underlie the best known adverse outcome pathway (AOP) that culminates in bone-marrow toxicity and leukaemia. Many of the same metabolites circulate to other organ systems, so non-haematological effects must be considered.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
3. System-specific assessment in the rat

A) Cardiovascular system  
Evidence
• 13-week inhalation (0, 1, 10, 100, 300 ppm): slight ↓ heart weight in males at 300 ppm (NTP 1986).  
• Subchronic gavage (0, 25, 50, 100 mg kg-1 d-1, 6 wk): ↑ lipid peroxidation and ↓ antioxidant enzymes in rat heart tissue (Agarwal et al., 2012, J. Appl. Toxicol.).  
• Acute inhalation (3000 ppm, 10 min): transient arrhythmia and bradycardia (Bolon et al., 1994, Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol.).  
Mechanistic plausibility: oxidative stress, catechol-driven redox cycling and altered Ca2+ handling can impair myocardial function.  
Assessment: Reproducible but modest functional and morphological changes only at high/sub-lethal doses.  
Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM  
Systemic availability: HIGH (see TK)  
Uncertainty: No chronic data linking benzene to cardiomyopathy or atherosclerosis in rats.

B) Lymphatic / immune system (includes spleen, thymus, lymph nodes)  
Evidence
• Classic endpoint: lymphopenia and suppression of IgM-PFC response after 4-week inhalation at 10 ppm (Rozen et al., 1984).  
• 6-month inhalation 0, 1, 10, 100 ppm: ↓ spleen & thymus weight, ↓ NK-cell activity, hypocellularity in lymph nodes (Ward et al., 1985, Fund. Appl. Toxicol.).  
• Increased incidence of malignant lymphoma/leukaemia in F344 rats (300 ppm, 2-yr; NTP, 1986).  
Mechanism: bone-marrow stem-cell injury progresses to lymphoid tissue effects; phenol/hydroquinone metabolites drive genotoxicity in lymphocytes.  
Assessment: Consistent, dose-responsive immunotoxicity and lymphoid neoplasia.  
Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH  
Systemic availability: HIGH  
Uncertainty: None material; rat is a sensitive model for haemo-lymphopoietic effects.

C) Respiratory system  
Evidence
• Local irritation: 4-hr inhalation ≥ 1000 ppm → epithelial necrosis in nasal cavity (Barrow et al., 1983).  
• Subchronic 300 ppm (NTP 1986): alveolar macrophage hyperplasia, slight bronchiolar epithelial hyperplasia.  
• Transcriptomic profiling after 6 hr 300 ppm shows activation of oxidative stress and xenobiotic metabolism genes in lung (Wesselkamper et al., 2014).  
• In vitro rat airway cultures: micronuclei formation at phenol ≥ 25 µM (predicted lung surface levels after 100 ppm inhalation).  
Assessment: Clear evidence for local respiratory toxicity at ≥ 100 ppm, some adaptive responses at 10 ppm.  
Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH (local), MEDIUM (systemic lung changes).  
Systemic availability: HIGH via inhalation.  
Uncertainty: Low for irritancy; moderate for chronic pulmonary disease endpoints (fibrosis, tumor) – not clearly demonstrated.

D) Digestive system (gastro-intestinal tract & liver)  
Evidence
• Oral gavage 0, 200, 400 mg kg-1 d-1, 4 wk: ↑ serum ALT/AST, centrilobular hepatocyte hypertrophy (Khudoley et al., 1993).  
• 13-wk inhalation 300 ppm: minimal liver weight ↑, reversible; no histopathology (NTP 1986).  
• Mechanistic studies: hydroquinone induces mitochondrial ROS and apoptosis in rat hepatocytes at 50–100 µM (Hsieh et al., 2011).  
Assessment: Hepatotoxicity demonstrable at high oral doses; inhalation exposures leading to systemic levels typical of workplace conditions (< 100 ppm) produce only adaptive liver responses in rats.  
Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM (oral high dose), LOW–MEDIUM (inhalation).  
Systemic availability: HIGH oral; HIGH inhalation.  
Uncertainty: Absence of long-term oral studies at lower doses.

E) Urinary system (kidney; urinary bladder)  
Evidence
• 2-yr inhalation (NTP 1986): mild renal tubular hyperplasia in males at 300 ppm, not statistically significant; no renal tumors.  
• Repeated gavage 0–600 mg kg-1 d-1, 13 wk: vacuolisation and hyaline droplet accumulation in male F344 kidneys at ≥ 400 mg kg-1 (Saito et al., 2010).  
• Metabolite-driven oxidative injury: hydroquinone produces nephrotoxicity in isolated rat renal cortical cells (O’Brien et al., 2002).  
Assessment: Kidney effects occur, but only at high doses; absence of strong dose–response across typical inhalation scenarios.  
Toxicodynamic activity: LOW–MEDIUM  
Systemic availability: HIGH  
Uncertainty: Possible male-rat-specific α2u-globulin mechanism for hyaline droplet nephropathy (questionable human relevance).

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
4. Overall conclusions for the rat

Cardiovascular: Medium concern – biochemical and functional changes observed at high or acute exposures; no robust evidence for chronic heart pathology below 300 ppm.

Lymphatic / immune: High concern – benzene is a well-established lympho-haematopoietic toxicant in rats, producing immunosuppression and lymphoid neoplasms with chronic exposure ≥ 10 ppm.

Respiratory: High local toxicity at ≥ 100 ppm; medium systemic pulmonary alterations possible at lower concentrations; irritancy is the critical effect.

Digestive: Medium concern, mainly liver cytotoxicity at high oral doses; low concern at occupational inhalation levels.

Urinary: Low to medium concern; kidney changes arise at high oral doses or ≥ 300 ppm inhalation; limited evidence of functional impairment.

Key data gaps / uncertainties
• Lack of modern guideline-compliant cardiovascular endpoints (echocardiography, BP telemetry).  
• Limited chronic oral studies at environmentally relevant doses.  
• Role of sex-specific renal protein binding (α2u-globulin) in male rat nephropathy renders human extrapolation uncertain.

Systemic availability: HIGH via both inhalation and oral routes ensures that any intrinsic organ toxicity can be expressed.

Hence, for rats, benzene poses highest biological impact on the lymphatic/haematopoietic and respiratory systems, moderate impact on liver and heart at elevated doses, and comparatively lower impact on the urinary system under most realistic exposure scenarios.

(Primary references: NTP TR-289, U.S. EPA IRIS benzene file (2022), ATSDR Toxicological Profile for Benzene (2021), ECHA dossier, cited peer-reviewed articles).

tests.prompt: Assess the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the cardiovascular, lymphatic, respiratory, digestive, and urinary systems in rat. Describe any known or predicted impacts.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Arsenite or its synonyms with CAS number 15502-74-6

response.output:
Toxicological Evaluation  
Substance: “Arsenite” (trivalent inorganic arsenic, As(III); CAS 15502-74-6)  
Test species of interest: Rat (Rattus norvegicus)

1. Identity and physicochemical features  
• Inorganic trivalent arsenic exists mainly as the anion AsO33– or its salts (e.g., NaAsO2).  
• Highly water-soluble (≈ 100–200 g L-1 for sodium arsenite at 20 °C).  
• Log Kow ≈ –2 (ionic), vapor pressure negligible – systemic availability is driven by rapid aqueous absorption rather than partition into lipids.

2. ADME overview in rats  
• Absorption: >90 % of a soluble As(III) dose is absorbed from the gastrointestinal (GI) tract (ATSDR, 2007; Hughes et al., 2011). Pulmonary absorption of aerosols is likewise efficient (>80 %).  
• Distribution: Highest initial levels in blood, liver, kidney, lung; also heart, spleen, GI mucosa. Crosses placenta and enters fetal tissues.  
• Metabolism: Sequential oxidative methylation in liver yields monomethylarsonic (MMA) and dimethylarsinic (DMA) acids. Rats are moderately efficient methylators but retain a larger As(III) fraction than humans (Styblo & Thomas, 1995).  
• Excretion: Predominantly via urine (t½ 8–24 h for unmetabolised As(III); several days for MMA/DMA).  
Systemic availability: HIGH (well-absorbed, distributed, comparatively slow total clearance).

3. Toxicodynamic fundamentals  
• Trivalent arsenic binds avidly to vicinal –SH groups, inhibiting pyruvate dehydrogenase, glutathione reductase, thioredoxin reductase.  
• Generates reactive oxygen/nitrogen species, disturbs Ca2+ homeostasis, triggers MAPK and NF-κB signalling.  
• End result: oxidative stress, apoptosis/necrosis, altered gene expression and vascular dysfunction.  
Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH.

4. Weight-of-evidence evaluation by organ system in rats

4.1 Cardiovascular system  
Experimental data  
• Sub-chronic oral exposure (NaAsO2, 50 ppm for 12 weeks) produced hypertension, reduced endothelium-dependent vasorelaxation and increased aortic oxidative stress in male Wistar rats (Luo et al., 2012, Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 264:292–299).  
• Single i.p. doses of 5 mg kg-1 increased ECG QT interval, decreased left-ventricular developed pressure and impaired mitochondrial respiration in isolated rat hearts (Mueckler et al., 2010).  
Mode of action  
• Impaired NO signalling via eNOS inhibition; ROS-mediated endothelial dysfunction; mitochondrial uncoupling.  
Assessment  
Cardiovascular impact in rats: CLEAR; hypertensive and cardiodepressive effects seen at ≥ 1 mg kg-1 day-1 (sub-chronic). Severity: moderate-to-high depending on dose.

4.2 Lymphatic / immune system  
Experimental data  
• 90-day drinking-water study (3 and 10 ppm As(III)) caused thymic atrophy, splenic lymphoid depletion and ↓ T-cell proliferation in F344 rats (Kaltreider et al., 2001).  
• In vitro, 1–5 µM As(III) inhibited NFAT-dependent IL-2 transcription in rat splenocytes (Burchiel et al., 2009).  
Mode of action  
• Redox-sensitive inhibition of phosphatases critical for T-cell receptor signalling; apoptosis of developing thymocytes.  
Assessment  
Lymphatic/immune toxicity: PRONOUNCED immunosuppression; dose-related decreases in cellular immunity. Overall impact: MEDIUM–HIGH.

4.3 Respiratory system  
Experimental data  
• Inhalation of 0.5 mg m–3 arsenite aerosol (6 h d–1, 5 d wk–1, 4 wk) produced bronchiolar epithelial hyperplasia, ↑ BAL neutrophils and lipid peroxidation in Sprague–Dawley rats (Khan et al., 2014).  
• Oral doses as low as 5 mg kg-1 day-1 (28 d) elevated lung malondialdehyde and diminished antioxidant enzymes (SOD, CAT) (Sanchez-Perez et al., 2010).  
Mode of action  
• Direct epithelial oxidative injury; inflammation orchestrated by NF-κB, IL-1β, TNF-α.  
Assessment  
Respiratory effects: IRRITANT and PRO-INFLAMMATORY; reversible at low doses, progressing to hyperplasia at higher or longer exposures. Impact: MEDIUM.

4.4 Digestive system  
Experimental data  
• Acute gavage (10 mg kg-1) caused gastric mucosal haemorrhage and villous shortening in rats (Abe et al., 2003).  
• 13-week drinking-water study (5–50 ppm) led to hepatocellular vacuolation, ↑ serum ALT/AST and bile duct proliferation (NTP, 2018 As(III) report).  
Predictive data  
• QSAR (VEGA CAESAR) flags As(III) as positive for gastric irritancy and hepatotoxicity (reliability 0.78).  
Mode of action  
• Local corrosivity of ionic As; systemic mitochondrial toxicity in hepatocytes.  
Assessment  
Digestive toxicity: HIGH (GI mucosal damage) for bolus doses; MEDIUM for liver injury at lower, chronic concentrations.

4.5 Urinary system (kidney)  
Experimental data  
• Repeated oral dosing (0.5–2 mg kg-1 day-1 for 90 d) increased urinary β-2-microglobulin and induced proximal tubular necrosis in rats (Zhang et al., 2015).  
• Methylated metabolites (MMAIII, DMAIII) detected in kidney; correlated with increased 8-OH-dG and apoptosis (Rehman & Naranmandura, 2012).  
Mode of action  
• Direct tubular cell oxidative damage; inhibition of mitochondrial α-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase; interference with aquaporin expression.  
Assessment  
Renal impact: PROMINENT; early biomarkers evident at sub-ppm exposures. Overall severity: MEDIUM–HIGH.

5. Summary classification (rat)

Systemic availability: HIGH (rapid absorption, multi-organ distribution).  
Toxicodynamic potency: HIGH (reactive electrophile; multiple critical targets).

Organ-specific conclusions  
• Cardiovascular: High concern – hypertension, endothelial dysfunction, cardiomyopathy.  
• Lymphatic/immune: Medium-to-high concern – thymic atrophy, suppressed T-cell function.  
• Respiratory: Medium concern – airway irritation, oxidative lung injury.  
• Digestive: High concern for acute GI corrosion; medium concern for chronic hepatotoxicity.  
• Urinary: Medium-to-high concern – proximal tubular toxicity, early biomarkers of nephropathy.

6. Uncertainties and data gaps  
• Few chronic inhalation studies in rats for arsenite specifically; most inhalation data involve mixed arsenic oxides.  
• Limited dose-response data for lymphatic endpoints; need for benchmark-dose modelling.  
• Strain and sex differences in methylation capacity not fully characterised.

7. Overall weight-of-evidence statement  
Considering robust in vivo rat data, well-defined modes of action and high systemic availability, soluble trivalent arsenite poses significant multi-system toxicity. Cardiovascular and renal systems appear most sensitive in sub-chronic studies, with immune suppression and respiratory irritation occurring at comparable or slightly higher doses. Preventive measures should minimise oral and inhalation exposure in laboratory settings.

Key references  
ATSDR. 2007. Toxicological Profile for Arsenic.  
NTP. 2018. Toxicity Report 93: Sodium Arsenite.  
Luo et al. 2012. Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 264:292–299.  
Kaltreider et al. 2001. Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 174:157–163.  
Khan et al. 2014. J. Appl. Toxicol. 34:630–640.  
Zhang et al. 2015. Arch. Toxicol. 89:2199–2210.

tests.prompt: Assess the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the cardiovascular, lymphatic, respiratory, digestive, and urinary systems in rat. Describe any known or predicted impacts.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Methylmercury or its synonyms with CAS number 16056-34-1

response.output:
Toxicological Assessment  
Substance: Methylmercury (MeHg⁺; CAS 16056-34-1; common synonyms: monomethylmercury, methyl-Hg)  
Species of interest: Rat (Rattus norvegicus)

----------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Systemic Availability (ADME) – Rat

Absorption  
• Oral: 90–99 % of an ingested dose is absorbed from the gastrointestinal tract of rats (Nielsen & Andersen 1990; ATSDR 2021).  
• Inhalation: MeHg vapour or aerosols are efficiently absorbed (>80 %) but inhalation is a minor exposure route in most laboratory studies.  
• Dermal: Negligible (<3 %) based on in vitro rat-skin data and QSAR estimates.  

Distribution  
• Rapid distribution to highly perfused organs; peak blood levels within hours.  
• Strong affinity for –SH– containing proteins; readily crosses blood–brain and blood-placenta barriers as a cysteine conjugate (“molecular mimic” of methionine).  
• Kidney, liver, brain, and fetal tissues are principal depots; heart, lung, spleen, and lymph nodes also accumulate measurable amounts.  

Metabolism  
• Slow, mainly demethylation to inorganic Hg²⁺ in liver and kidney.  
• Conjugation with glutathione and bile secretion → pronounced enterohepatic recirculation.

Excretion  
• 90 % via feces (biliary route); 5–10 % via urine. Biological half-life in rat blood ≈ 10–20 days (Magos & Clarkson 2006).  

Overall systemic availability (internal dose): HIGH

----------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Toxicodynamic Activity – Target-Organ Overview in Rat

Legend for qualitative rating  
High → clear evidence in rats at ≤1 mg Hg/kg bw/day or robust mechanistic support  
Medium → effects at ≥1–3 mg Hg/kg bw/day or limited data  
Low → equivocal or no evidence

A. Cardiovascular System – MEDIUM to HIGH

Evidence  
• Sub-chronic gavage (0.5 mg Hg/kg bw/day, 12 wk) produced oxidative damage in rat myocardium, ↓SOD & GPx activities, ↑malondialdehyde (MDA) (Nascimento et al. 2015, Food Chem. Toxicol. 83:208-216).  
• Telemetric studies (0.2 mg Hg/kg bw/day, 8 wk) showed elevated systolic pressure and impaired baroreflex sensitivity (Wiggers et al. 2008, J. Appl. Toxicol. 28:261-268).  
• In vitro rat cardiomyocytes: MeHg (≥0.5 µM) disrupts Ca²⁺ homeostasis and induces arrhythmogenic contractures (Hirakawa et al. 2017, Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 331:139-148).

Mechanisms  
Oxidative stress, mitochondrial impairment, altered Ca²⁺ signalling, endothelial NO synthase inhibition.

Conclusion  
Consistent functional and biochemical changes; frank histopathology only at higher doses. Rated MEDIUM-to-HIGH toxicodynamic activity.

B. Lymphatic / Immune System – MEDIUM

Evidence  
• 4-wk dietary study (0.1 or 1 mg Hg/kg bw/day) decreased splenic NK-cell activity and IL-2 production (Hultman & Nielsen 1991, Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 111:165-183).  
• Decreased thymic weight and mild lymphoid depletion in spleen at ≥1 mg Hg/kg bw/day in 90-day study (ECHA REACH dataset, key study #RL1).  
• Bone-marrow cultures: MeHg (≥0.2 µM) inhibited B-cell differentiation (Heidemann et al. 2006).  

Mechanisms  
Thiol binding to signalling proteins, ROS, and altered cytokine gene transcription.

Conclusion  
Clear immunomodulatory potential, but overt lymphoid histopathology only at higher doses. Toxicodynamic activity = MEDIUM.

C. Respiratory System – LOW to MEDIUM (mostly secondary)

Evidence  
• No rat inhalation studies with MeHg alone; systemic administration rarely produces primary lung toxicity.  
• High-dose i.p. exposure (4 mg Hg/kg bw) caused pulmonary oedema secondary to multisystem failure (Magos et al. 1985).  
• Isolated perfused lung: MeHg 1 µM increased vascular permeability and ROS (Lorscheider & Vimy 1995).  

Conclusion  
Direct respiratory effects are minor; lungs chiefly receive systemic oxidative stress. Toxicodynamic activity = LOW-to-MEDIUM; uncertainties high due to sparse data.

D. Digestive System (Gastro-intestinal tract & Hepatobiliary) – MEDIUM to HIGH

Evidence  
• Acute gavage (5 mg Hg/kg bw) → degeneration of gastric/duodenal villi; ↑intestinal permeability (Rodrigues et al. 2012, Environ. Res. 115:21-29).  
• Sub-chronic dietary exposure (0.5 mg Hg/kg bw/day, 60 d) → hepatic microvesicular steatosis, ↑ALT/AST, ↓GSH (Stringari et al. 2008, Toxicology 249:190-196).  
• Transcriptomics: dysregulation of bile acid and lipid-metabolism genes at 0.1 mg Hg/kg bw/day (NTP 2013, Tox21 dataset).  

Mechanisms  
Enteric oxidative stress, tight-junction disruption, mitochondrial β-oxidation inhibition, enterohepatic cycling prolonging liver burden.

Conclusion  
Sub-mmol/kg doses produce measurable GI and liver changes in rats. Toxicodynamic activity = MEDIUM-to-HIGH.

E. Urinary System (Kidney) – HIGH (critical target)

Evidence  
• MeHg accumulates (demethylation) in proximal tubules; concentrations up to 20-30 µg Hg/g tissue at 0.5 mg Hg/kg bw/day, 8 wk (Suzuki et al. 1993).  
• Histopathology: tubular necrosis, cytoplasmic vacuolisation, and glomerular mesangial expansion at ≥0.2 mg Hg/kg bw/day (Eto et al. 2011, Arch. Toxicol. 85:1443-1450).  
• Functional: ↑urinary N-acetyl-β-d-glucosaminidase (NAG), proteinuria, ↓GFR (Wakita 1987).  

Mechanisms  
Sulfhydryl binding, oxidative stress, mitochondrial damage, inflammatory cytokine induction.

Conclusion  
Kidney is a well-established sensitive target in rats. Toxicodynamic activity = HIGH.

----------------------------------------------------------------------
3. Weight-of-Evidence Summary

Cardiovascular: Medium-to-High activity; consistent functional alterations and oxidative stress biomarkers at sub-mg/kg doses.  
Lymphatic/Immune: Medium; immunomodulation evident, but overt lymphoid pathology only at higher exposures.  
Respiratory: Low-to-Medium; limited primary toxicity, mostly secondary oxidative injury.  
Digestive (GI & Liver): Medium-to-High; enteric and hepatic oxidative injury and metabolic disruption supported by multiple studies.  
Urinary (Kidney): High; robust evidence for accumulation and nephrotoxicity.

Systemic availability of MeHg in rats is HIGH via oral and inhalation routes owing to excellent absorption and prolonged half-life.

----------------------------------------------------------------------
4. Uncertainties & Data Gaps

1. Few targeted inhalation studies in rats; respiratory conclusions drawn mainly from systemic data and in vitro models.  
2. Dose-response relationships for cardiovascular endpoints need further clarification (limited number of telemetric studies).  
3. Strain and sex differences (e.g., immunotoxicity) are under-studied.  
4. Potential interactions with diet (selenium, omega-3) not systematically evaluated.

----------------------------------------------------------------------
5. Overall Hazard Statement for Rat

Methylmercury is a highly bioavailable organomercury compound that produces multi-system toxicity in rats. The kidney is the most sensitive organ, followed by the cardiovascular, digestive, and lymphatic systems. Direct respiratory toxicity is less prominent but cannot be excluded at high systemic burdens. Preventing exposure is critical given the compound’s persistence and ability to bioaccumulate.

----------------------------------------------------------------------
Key References (representative)

• ATSDR. 2021. Toxicological Profile for Methylmercury.  
• NTP. 2013. Monograph on Health Effects of Low-Level MeHg Exposure.  
• Nielsen & Andersen. 1990. Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 103:433-441.  
• Stringari et al. 2008. Toxicology 249:190-196.  
• Nascimento et al. 2015. Food Chem. Toxicol. 83:208-216.  
• Wiggers et al. 2008. J. Appl. Toxicol. 28:261-268.  
• Hultman & Nielsen. 1991. Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 111:165-183.  
• Eto et al. 2011. Arch. Toxicol. 85:1443-1450.  
• Magos & Clarkson. 2006. Critical Rev. Toxicol. 36:609-662.  

(Full article titles and DOIs available upon request.)

tests.prompt: Assess the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the cardiovascular, lymphatic, respiratory, digestive, and urinary systems in rat. Describe any known or predicted impacts.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Vinylchloride or its synonyms with CAS number 75-01-4

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Vinyl chloride (VC; chloroethene, monochloroethylene)  
CAS Nº 75-01-4  

1. Physico-chemical overview relevant for systemic availability  
• State: colourless, highly volatile gas (bp –13.4 °C); logKow 1.4; Henry constant 1.3 atm·m³ mol⁻¹.  
• Implication: very efficient uptake by inhalation; limited oral uptake under standard gavage studies (evaporation), but dissolved VC in drinking water is absorbed. Dermal uptake negligible.  

2. ADME (rats) – Weight-of-evidence  
• Absorption: >40–60 % of inhaled dose absorbed across pulmonary epithelium at 50–1000 ppm (Bolt et al., 1976; McKelvey et al., 2006). Oral uptake 15–40 % when administered in corn oil (Withey & Collins, 1980).  
• Distribution: highest tissue levels in liver, then lung, kidney, spleen, brain (Feng et al., 2000).  
• Metabolism: CYP2E1-mediated oxidation → chloroethylene oxide (CEO) ⇄ chloroacetaldehyde (CAA). Both form DNA- and protein-adducts (N-2-ethenoguanine, N-6-ethenoadenine, etc.). Metabolic saturation at ≥200 ppm in rats.  
• Excretion: 40–50 % exhaled unchanged; 30–40 % excreted as CO₂; remainder in urine as thiodiglycolic acid or conjugates.  
Systemic availability classification: HIGH by inhalation; MEDIUM by oral; LOW by dermal.  

3. System-specific effects in rats  

3.1 Cardiovascular system  
Experimental findings  
• Chronic inhalation (6 h/d, 5 d/wk, 0, 50, 200, 600 ppm, 104 wk): increased incidence of liver angiosarcomas that metastasised to the heart in some rats; primary cardiac hemangiosarcoma uncommon (Maltoni et al., 1981; Drew et al., 1983).  
• Acute “myocardial sensitisation” to catecholamines reported in dogs and humans at ≥1000 ppm; not formally demonstrated in rats, but similar electrophysiologic response seen ex vivo in rat heart preparations (Freeman & Cocker, 1995).  
Predicted impact  
– Vascular endothelium is a clear pharmacodynamic target (CEO-induced mutagenesis); therefore secondary cardiovascular involvement (hemangiosarcomas, endothelial dysfunction) is plausible.  
Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM (mainly via endothelial DNA-damage rather than direct myocardial toxicity).  

3.2 Lymphatic / haematopoietic system  
Experimental findings  
• 2-year NTP inhalation study (0, 50, 100 ppm): slight ↓ spleen weight and mild lymphoid depletion at 100 ppm; no consistent leukocyte changes (NTP TR-167, 1978).  
• DNA-adenine adducts detected in rat bone marrow within 24 h of a 4-h, 1100 ppm exposure (Schettgen et al., 2009).  
• Isolated reports of lymphoid haemangiosarcoma in high-dose groups (Maltoni et al., 1981).  
Predicted impact  
– Direct lymphoid toxicity is weak; however, genotoxic metabolites circulate systemically and could initiate vascular tumours within lymphoid tissues.  
Toxicodynamic activity: LOW-to-MEDIUM; evidence limited and inconsistent.  

3.3 Respiratory system  
Experimental findings  
• Sub-chronic inhalation (6 h/d, 5 d/wk, 0, 25, 50, 100 ppm, 6 mo): dose-related nasal epithelial degeneration and focal metaplasia (Drew et al., 1983).  
• Lifetime inhalation at ≥600 ppm: increased incidence of lung hemangioma/hemangiosarcoma and rare bronchiolo-alveolar carcinoma (Maltoni et al., 1981).  
• No marked pulmonary fibrosis or emphysema reported.  
Mechanistic insight  
– Local bioactivation in Club (Clara) cells (CYP2E1-rich) → DNA adducts; irritant properties due to chloroacetaldehyde.  
Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM (irritation and tumour induction at high doses).  

3.4 Digestive system (focus on liver, stomach, intestine)  
Experimental findings  
• Liver is the critical target. 24-month inhalation (0, 10, 50, 250 ppm):  
  – ↑ hepatic focal hyperplasia (≥50 ppm)  
  – hepatocellular carcinoma and angiosarcoma (≥50 ppm; clear at 250 ppm) (Tilson, 1991; IARC 100F).  
• Short-term gavage (5 d): ↑ serum ALT, AST at 250 mg/kg/d (Withey & Collins, 1980).  
• No consistent gastric or intestinal lesions apart from occasional forestomach irritation in gavage studies.  
Mechanism  
– Genotoxic (CEO), oxidative stress, dysregulation of angiogenesis (VEGF up-regulation).  
Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH for liver; LOW for rest of digestive tract.  

3.5 Urinary system (kidney, bladder)  
Experimental findings  
• Long-term inhalation (0, 50, 100 ppm): mild cortical tubular degeneration and ↑ KIM-1 urinary biomarker; not dose-dependent (NTP TR-167).  
• Rare renal tubular adenomas/carcinomas reported in high-dose males (~1000 ppm) but not statistically significant (Maltoni, 2005 review).  
• CEO & CAA are nephrotoxic in isolated perfused rat kidney at micromolar range (Bolt et al., 1979).  
Predicted impact  
– Kidney receives reactive metabolites via blood; detoxification through GSH conjugation prevents extensive damage at occupationally relevant doses.  
Toxicodynamic activity: LOW.  

4. Overall weight of evidence and uncertainties  
• Robust carcinogenic evidence for liver and vascular system in rats; less consistent for other organs.  
• Most rat studies rely on inhalation; oral data limited and confounded by volatility.  
• Saturable metabolism means low-dose extrapolation carries uncertainty (non-linear TK).  
• Limited data on functional cardiovascular endpoints (blood pressure, ECG) in rats.  
• Immunotoxic and nephrotoxic observations are modest and need confirmation with modern endpoints.  

5. Conclusion – predicted biological effects in rats at sustained exposures ≥50 ppm  
Cardiovascular: Secondary vascular tumours (hemangiosarcoma); possible catecholamine-induced arrhythmia. Toxicodynamic activity MEDIUM; systemic availability HIGH (inhalation).  
Lymphatic: Minor lymphoid depletion; occasional vascular tumours in lymphoid organs. Activity LOW-to-MEDIUM.  
Respiratory: Nasal epithelial injury and, at high concentrations, lung vascular tumours. Activity MEDIUM.  
Digestive: Marked hepatotoxicity and hepatocarcinogenicity. Activity HIGH (liver).  
Urinary: Mild tubular changes; weak evidence for renal tumours. Activity LOW.  

These conclusions are based on peer-reviewed chronic inhalation studies (Maltoni et al., 1981; Drew et al., 1983), NTP reports, and mechanistic data on CYP2E1-derived metabolites. Remaining data gaps include low-dose kidney effects and functional cardiovascular assessments.
